## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the salient features of the Environmental (Protection) Act, 1986. Mention its constitutional basis.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) Enacted under **Article 253** of the Constitution after the **Stockholm Conference (1972)**; also called an "umbrella" Act because it covers all environmental matters not specifically dealt with by Water (1974) or Air (1981) Acts.
- (1 mark) Provides powers to the **Central Government** to coordinate state actions, regulate industrial sites, prescribe standards, and conduct research.
- (1 mark) Establishes **CPCB and SPCBs** as implementing agencies; **penalties up to 5 years imprisonment / ₹1 lakh fine** + continuing fine of ₹5,000/day; offences are **cognizable and bailable**.

---

#### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** What is NAAQS? List any four pollutants with their 24-hour limits.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **NAAQS = National Ambient Air Quality Standards** notified by **CPCB in 2009** under Section 6 of EPA 1986; specify permissible concentration of air pollutants in ambient atmosphere to protect human health and vegetation.
- (2 marks) Four pollutants (24-hr): **PM₁₀ = 100 µg/m³; PM₂.₅ = 60 µg/m³; SO₂ = 80 µg/m³; NO₂ = 80 µg/m³** (any four accepted).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **Environmental (Protection) Act, 1986** with reference to its objectives, salient features, powers of the Central Government, and the role of CPCB and SPCBs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Water Quality Standards** prescribed in India under EPA 1986. List the parameters of **IS 10500:2012** and explain the significance of TDS, Fluoride, Nitrate, and Total Coliforms. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) EPA 1986 — Detailed Discussion (7 marks)**

- **Background (1 mark):** Enacted in 1986 (came into force 23 May 1986) under Article 253 after the Bhopal Gas Tragedy (Dec 1984); Acts such as Water (1974) and Air (1981) had specific scope, leaving a legislative gap.
- **Objectives (1 mark):** (i) Implement decisions of Stockholm 1972, (ii) Coordinate state action, (iii) Lay down standards, (iv) Regulate discharge of pollutants, (v) Safeguard against industrial accidents.
- **Salient Features (2 marks):**
  - Umbrella Act — applies to whole of India and any substance/matrix not covered by earlier laws.
  - Defines *environment* very broadly (water, air, land, inter-relationship, living beings).
  - Empowers Central Government to delegate powers (Section 4).
- **Powers (2 marks):**
  - **Section 3** – power to take all measures to protect/improve environment.
  - **Section 5** – binding directions to any person, officer or authority.
  - **Section 6** – power to make rules prescribing standards (NAAQS, noise rules, effluent schedules, hazardous waste rules).
- **Role of CPCB/SPCB (1 mark):** CPCB advises Central Government and coordinates SPCBs; SPCBs enforce standards within the state through consent orders, sampling, prosecution under Sections 9/10.

**(b) Water Quality Standards — IS 10500:2012 (7 marks)**

- **Concept (1 mark):** IS 10500:2012 is the **Bureau of Indian Standards** specification for drinking water, notified under EPA 1986 Section 6. Two-tier limits: **Desirable** (no adverse effect) and **Permissible in absence of alternate source** (short-term tolerable).
- **Significance of parameters (4 marks, 1 each):**
  - **TDS (500 / 2000 mg/L):** Palatability; high TDS causes gastrointestinal irritation and indicates dissolved mineral contamination.
  - **Fluoride (1.0 / 1.5 mg/L):** Both deficiency and excess cause disease — deficiency → dental caries; excess (>1.5 mg/L) → **dental and skeletal fluorosis**.
  - **Nitrate (45 mg/L, no relaxation):** Excess nitrate in drinking water causes **methaemoglobinaemia (blue-baby syndrome)** in infants.
  - **Total Coliforms (0 / 0 MPN/100 mL):** Indicator of **faecal (sewage) contamination** → risk of water-borne pathogens (E. coli, Vibrio, Salmonella).
- **Enforcement (1 mark):** Public health engineering department (PHED) / Kerala Water Authority monitors compliance; SPCB can prosecute offenders under EPA Section 9.
- **Other notable parameters (1 mark):** pH (6.5–8.5), Total Hardness (200/600 mg/L), Iron (0.3 mg/L), Chloride (250/1000 mg/L), Sulphate (200/400 mg/L).

---

#### **Question B (14 marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 / Supplementary Examiner Set]**

**(a)** Explain the **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS), 2009** notified under EPA 1986. List any six criteria pollutants with their limits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Ambient Air Quality Index (AQI)**? Derive the **sub-index formula** and calculate the AQI if the 24-hr concentrations measured are: **PM₁₀ = 180 µg/m³, PM₂.₅ = 65 µg/m³, SO₂ = 90 µg/m³, NO₂ = 95 µg/m³**. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution

**(a) NAAQS 2009 (7 marks)**

- (1 mark) **Genesis:** First Indian NAAQS were notified in **1982 (CPCB)**; revised in **1994** and finally in **2009**, notified under Section 6 of EPA 1986 and Rule 3 of EPA Rules 1986.
- (1 mark) **Objective:** Specify permissible concentration of criteria pollutants in ambient air so that public health, vegetation and property are protected, with adequate safety margin.
- (1 mark) **12 pollutants covered:** PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, Pb, NH₃, Benzene, Benzo(a)Pyrene, Arsenic, Nickel.
- (4 marks) **Six criteria pollutants with limits (any six from the table below — ½ mark each for name, ½ mark each for correct limits):**

| Pollutant | Annual µg/m³ | 24-h µg/m³ | 8-h | 1-h |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PM₂.₅ | 40 | 60 | — | — |
| PM₁₀ | 60 | 100 | — | — |
| SO₂ | 50 | 80 | — | — |
| NO₂ | 40 | 80 | — | — |
| CO (mg/m³) | — | — | 02 | 04 |
| Pb | 0.50 | — | — | — |

**(b) AQI — Sub-index formula & numerical (7 marks)**

- **Definition (1 mark):** AQI is a single composite number that summarises the ambient concentrations of measured criteria pollutants into an easy-to-understand scale **0–500** (CPCB India AQI).
- **Formula derivation (3 marks):** As derived in Section 3.1 above: $I_p = \frac{I_{Hi}-I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi}-BP_{Lo}}(C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$, and $AQI = \max(I_p)$.
- **Numerical computation (3 marks):** For each pollutant, use CPCB 24-hr breakpoints:

For **PM₁₀ = 180 µg/m³** (BP 180→250 → I 100→200):

$$
I_{PM_{10}} = \frac{200-100}{250-180}(180-180) + 100 = 100
$$

For **PM₂.₅ = 65 µg/m³** (BP 60→90 → I 100→200):

$$
I_{PM_{2.5}} = \frac{200-100}{90-60}(65-60) + 100 = \frac{100}{30}\cdot 5 + 100 \approx 116.7
$$

For **SO₂ = 90 µg/m³** (BP 80→365 → I 100→200 for the CPCB-SaferAir-AQI table, using 24-hr):

$$
I_{SO_2} = \frac{200-100}{365-80}(90-80) + 100 = \frac{100}{285}\cdot 10 + 100 \approx 103.5
$$

For **NO₂ = 95 µg/m³** (BP 80→180 → I 100→200 for 24-hr):

$$
I_{NO_2} = \frac{200-100}{180-80}(95-80) + 100 = \frac{100}{100}\cdot 15 + 100 = 115
$$

- **Final AQI** (max of 100, 116.7, 103.5, 115) = **≈ 117 → "Moderate" category**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout (Do Not Lose Marks Here):**
> (1) Students often forget to **take the maximum** of all sub-indices — overall AQI is the max, not the average.
> (2) Always use the **correct breakpoint table for the corresponding averaging period** (annual ≠ 24-h ≠ 8-h).
> (3) In noise addition problems, students use **arithmetic addition** (e.g., 80+80=160) — this is **WRONG**. Always convert to linear intensity (10^(L/10)), sum, then convert back. Two identical 80 dB(A) sources give **83 dB(A)**, not 160.
> (4) For water standards, students quote wrong units (mg/L vs ppm — both identical for water; ml vs g). **Fluoride permissible limit is 1.5 mg/L** (not 1.0 — 1.0 is desirable).
> (5) The 24-hr NAAQS for CO is **4 mg/m³**, but the 8-hr value is **2 mg/m³** — do not transpose them.
> (6) Silence zone limit is **50/40 dB(A)**, **not** 55/45 — silence zone is stricter than residential.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — EPA 1986 & Standards**

- **EPA 1986 = Umbrella Act** under Article 253; came into force 23 May 1986; administered by **MoEFCC + CPCB + SPCBs**.
- **Key Sections:** 3 (protect), 4 (officers), 5 (directions), 6 (rules & standards), 7-8 (information/hazardous), 9 (penalty 5 yr + ₹1 lakh), 10 (company liability), 13 (cognizance by complaint only).
- **Drinking Water (IS 10500:2012):** pH 6.5–8.5; TDS 500/2000; Fluoride 1.0/1.5; Nitrate 45 (no relaxation); Total Coliforms 0/0 MPN/100 mL.
- **Surface Water (CPCB):** Class A→F — DO 6→; BOD 2→; Coliforms 50→5000; pH 6.5–8.5.
- **NAAQS 2009 — Critical 24-hr Limits:** PM₂.₅ = 60; PM₁₀ = 100; SO₂ = 80; NO₂ = 80; CO = 4 mg/m³ (1-h) / 2 mg/m³ (8-h); O₃ = 180 µg/m³ (1-h) / 100 (8-h); Pb annual = 0.5 µg/m³; NH₃ 24-h = 400 µg/m³; Benzene annual = 5 µg/m³.
- **AQI:** Piecewise linear interpolation; **overall AQI = MAX of sub-indices**; categories 0–50 Good → 401–500 Severe.
- **Noise Standards (dB(A)):**
  - Industrial **75/70**, Commercial **65/55**, Residential **55/45**, Silence **50/40** (day/night).
- **Noise math:** $L_{tot}=10\log_{10}\sum 10^{L_i/10}$ — doubling identical sources = **+3 dB(A)**.
- **Other important Rules notified under EPA 1986:** Hazardous Waste M&H Rules 2016, E-Waste Rules 2022, Bio-Medical Waste Rules 2016, Plastic Waste Rules 2022 (amended), Construction & Demolition Waste Rules 2016, Batteries (M&R) Rules 2022, e-Waste (M) Rules 2022.
- **Special facts:** EPA 1986 has **no citizen-suit provision** — prosecutions must be filed by CPCB/SPCB/authorised officers (Section 13).
- **Pollution Appellate Authority** hears appeals against SPCB consent orders.
- **Penalty upgrade:** Amendment in 2024 increased imprisonment up to 7 years and fine up to ₹25 crore for environmental damage (retain for advanced KTU questions).
- **Airshed concept** under NAAQS for non-attainment cities (Delhi NCR, Mumbai, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EIA Notification 1994 - EIA Revised Notification 2006
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT632 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** EIA Notification 1994 - EIA Revised Notification 2006

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an EIA Notification?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EIA Notification** is a legally binding executive instrument issued by the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF&CC), Government of India**, under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**, which makes **Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA)** and **Environmental Clearance (EC)** mandatory for specified categories of industrial, infrastructural, mining, and developmental projects before their establishment or expansion.

In simpler terms, an EIA Notification is essentially a **"rulebook"** issued by the Central Government that tells project proponents (developers, industries, mining companies): *"You cannot start this type of project until you have scientifically assessed its environmental consequences, presented them to the public, and obtained official clearance from the government."*

## 1.2 The Two Defining Notifications of Indian EIA History

| Notification | Year of Issue | Salient Character |
|---|---|---|
| **EIA Notification, 1994** | 27 January 1994 | **First** statutory framework mandating EIA in India |
| **EIA Revised Notification, 2006** | 14 September 2006 | **Comprehensive replacement** that operationalized EIA with sectoral thresholds and structured clearance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why study both together?** The 2006 Notification is best understood as a **logical evolution** of the 1994 Notification. Every major amendment in 2006 was, in effect, a response to a specific limitation or gap identified in the 1994 framework. KTU examiners frequently ask **comparative questions** between the two.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Driving Licence for Industries"

Imagine a country where there were **no driving licences**, and anyone could drive a truck through a crowded market. People get hurt, buildings get damaged, and chaos prevails. One day, the government issues:

- **Rule 1 (1994):** "All big vehicles must be tested for safety before being driven on the road." — This is the **EIA Notification 1994**. It set the broad principle but lacked details.
- **Rule 2 (2006):** "All vehicles above a certain weight must obtain a fitness certificate; two-wheelers below a certain size need not; heavy trucks need an additional pollution certificate; tests must happen within 30 days." — This is the **EIA Revised Notification 2006**. It made the rule **specific, measurable, and enforceable**.

Just as driving rules were tightened to reduce accidents, the EIA Notifications were tightened to reduce **environmental damage from unregulated industrialization**.

## 1.4 Legal Parentage — Source of Authority

Both notifications derive their legal power from a **single Parent Act**:

$$\text{EIA Notification} \;\xleftarrow[\text{Delegated Legislation}]{\text{Issued under}} \;\text{Environment (Protection) Act, 1986} \;\xrightarrow[\text{Authority}]{\text{Executive}} \;\text{Article 253 + Entry 13, List I, Schedule VII, Constitution of India}$$

The 1986 Act (commonly called **EPA, 1986**) was an "umbrella Act" passed in the aftermath of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 1984)**. Section 3(1) and Section 3(2)(v) of the EPA empower the Central Government to frame rules for *"protecting and improving the quality of the environment and preventing, controlling and abating environmental pollution."* The EIA Notifications are the operational children of this provision.

## 1.5 Why These Notifications Matter for KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Module 1 foundation topic** — forms the legal backbone for all subsequent EIA studies.
> - Frequently tested as **2-mark definition**, **7-mark comparative**, or **14-mark analytical question**.
> - Students must remember **year, issuing authority, legal basis, and key procedural innovations** of each.
> - Always mention **MoEF&CC (now MoEF&CC — Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change)** as the issuing ministry.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The EIA Notification, 1994 — Deep Structural Analysis

### 2.1.1 Genesis and Background

Until 1994, India had no mandatory national EIA mechanism. Individual ministries issued ad-hoc environmental guidelines. Following the **Rio Earth Summit (1992)** and growing domestic pressure, the Government of India, through **MoEF (now MoEF&CC)**, issued the first comprehensive notification on **27 January 1994**.

### 2.1.2 Core Provisions of EIA Notification 1994

The 1994 Notification was relatively short (only a few pages) but revolutionary. Its **core structured logic** can be broken down into the following bullets:

- **Scope of Application:** Mandated EC for **29 categories** of projects (industries, mining, thermal power, ports, highways, etc.).
- **Threshold:** The notification listed specific industries but **did not clearly specify the size/area thresholds** for many activities. For example, all new industrial units in listed categories required clearance, but the size cut-off was ambiguous.
- **Authority:** Environmental Clearance granted by the **Central Government (MoEF)** for all projects — there was no State-level clearance mechanism.
- **EIA Report Requirement:** Project proponents had to submit an **EIA Report / Environmental Management Plan (EMP)** prepared by competent consultants.
- **Public Hearing:** Public consultation was introduced but had **vague procedural guidelines** (no fixed time limit, no formal methodology prescribed in the notification itself).
- **Validity:** EC validity was not explicitly time-bound; once granted, it was largely perpetual unless revoked.
- **Coverage Gaps:**
  - Did not cover **construction projects** (highways, townships, tourism) comprehensively.
  - Did not specifically cover **modern sectors** like IT parks, Special Economic Zones (SEZs), biotechnology.
  - No clear distinction between **expansion vs. modernization** vs. **new projects**.
  - **No state-level EIA authority** was defined.

### 2.1.3 Critical Limitations of the 1994 Notification

> [!WARNING]
> The 1994 notification, though path-breaking, suffered from **serious implementation gaps**:
> 1. **Centralized bottleneck** — every project, regardless of size, came to Delhi.
> 2. **No screening mechanism** — small and large projects treated equally.
> 3. **Sectoral thresholds absent** — ambiguity on "what size project needs full EIA?"
> 4. **Public hearing procedural weakness** — no standard format.
> 5. **No post-clearance monitoring** — clearance once granted was rarely verified on ground.

These limitations, and the demand for procedural clarity, led to the 2006 revision.

## 2.2 The EIA Revised Notification, 2006 — Deep Structural Analysis

Issued on **14 September 2006** and made effective from **14 September 2006 itself**, this notification superseded the 1994 framework and remains (with subsequent amendments) the **current operating document** of India's EIA regime.

### 2.2.1 Architectural Innovations of the 2006 Notification

The 2006 Notification can be decoded through the following hierarchical logic:

1. **Project Categorization — Category A and Category B**
   - **Category A:** Projects requiring **Central-level clearance** by MoEF&CC. These are large-scale, environmentally sensitive, or located in ecologically fragile areas.
   - **Category B:** Projects requiring **State-level clearance** by the **State Environment Impact Assessment Authority (SEIAA)**. These are smaller or moderately impacting projects.

2. **Sub-categorization of Category B — B1 and B2**
   - **Category B1:** Projects requiring **full EIA Report + EMP + Public Hearing**.
   - **Category B2:** Projects not requiring full EIA; they only require a **brief "Form-1 / Form-1A"** self-declaration with site-specific EMP.
   - The decision of B1 vs. B2 is made by **SEIAA** based on screening.

3. **Sectoral Thresholds Introduced**
   - Each project category now has **explicit quantitative thresholds** based on area, capacity, investment, or production. For example:
     - Mining: based on mining lease area (≥ 5 Ha → Category B, ≥ 50 Ha → Category A in some cases).
     - Thermal Power: based on MW capacity.
     - Highways: based on length and width.

4. **Standardized Procedural Timeframe**
   - **Application → ToR (Terms of Reference):** within **60 days**.
   - **Public Hearing:** within **45 days** of ToR.
   - **EIA Report submission → EC decision:** within **105 days**.
   - **Total clear timeline: ~150 days** for Category B; slightly longer for Category A.

5. **Establishment of SEIAA and SEAC**
   - Every State/UT had to constitute a **State Environment Impact Assessment Authority (SEIAA)** and a **State Expert Appraisal Committee (SEAC)** — decentralizing the process.

6. **Validity of EC Introduced**
   - EC validity: **5 years for river valley projects**, **7 years for others** for commencement of operations.

7. **Public Hearing — 2006 Notification Standardized the Process**
   - Conducted by **State Pollution Control Board (SPCB)**.
   - **30 days prior notice** mandatory.
   - **Verbatim record** of proceedings maintained.
   - **District-level accessibility** ensured.

8. **Coverage Expansion**
   - Number of categories increased from **29 (1994) to 39 (2006)**, including new sectors like **SEZs, biotechnology parks, petroleum refining, national highways, and large townships**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Reference Table

The following consolidated table captures **all critical parameters** required for KTU 2024 Scheme answer-writing on this topic. **Memorize this table.**

| Parameter | EIA Notification 1994 | EIA Revised Notification 2006 |
|---|---|---|
| **Date of Issue** | 27 January 1994 | 14 September 2006 |
| **Issuing Authority** | Ministry of Environment & Forests (MoEF) | Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF&CC) |
| **Legal Basis** | Sec. 3(1), 3(2)(v) of EPA, 1986 | Sec. 3(1), 3(2)(v) of EPA, 1986 |
| **Number of Project Categories** | 29 categories | 39 categories (expanded) |
| **Project Classification** | Single unified category | **Category A** (Central) & **Category B** (State); B further split into **B1 & B2** |
| **Clearance Authority** | Central Government (MoEF only) | **MoEF&CC** for Cat-A; **SEIAA** for Cat-B |
| **Sectoral Thresholds** | Not clearly defined | **Clearly specified** (e.g., area, MW, length, capacity) |
| **Public Hearing** | Mandatory but procedure vague | **Mandatory, standardized** — 30 days notice, 45-day window |
| **Screening Stage** | Absent | **Explicit Screening Stage** to determine Cat-A / B1 / B2 |
| **Scoping Stage (ToR)** | Informal / absent | **Formal Terms of Reference (ToR)** issued within **60 days** |
| **Validity of EC** | Not explicitly defined | **5 years** (river valley) / **7 years** (others) |
| **Post-Clearance Monitoring** | Weak / undefined | **Mandatory 6-monthly compliance reports** via PARIVESH portal |
| **Decentralization** | No state authority | **SEIAA & SEAC** in every State/UT |
| **Coverage of Construction Sector** | Partial | Comprehensive (townships, highways, SEZs, airports) |
| **EC Timeline** | Not time-bound | **~150 days (Category B)** |

## 2.4 Procedural Steps Under the 2006 Notification — High-Yield Flow

The 2006 Notification introduced a **four-stage process** that every project proponent must follow:

$$\boxed{\text{Stage 1: Screening} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Stage 2: Scoping (ToR)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Stage 3: Public Hearing \& EIA Report} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Stage 4: Appraisal \& EC Decision}}$$

- **Stage 1 — Screening:** SEIAA / MoEF&CC determines whether the project falls under A, B1, or B2.
- **Stage 2 — Scoping:** Terms of Reference (ToR) issued specifying what the EIA study must cover. Maximum **60 days**.
- **Stage 3 — Public Hearing + EIA Report:** Conducted by SPCB; EIA + EMP report submitted.
- **Stage 4 — Appraisal:** Expert Appraisal Committee (EAC for A; SEAC for B) examines the report, and SEIAA/MoEF&CC grants or rejects EC within **105 days**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, you should be able to answer the question **"Why is the 2006 Notification more engineer-relevant than 1994?"**
>
> **Answer:** Because the 2006 Notification introduced **engineer-decodable parameters** — capacity (MW for thermal power), area (hectares for mining), length (km for highways). This allows civil, mechanical, electrical, and mining engineers to **determine EC applicability directly from project design parameters** without legal interpretation. It also introduced the **PARIVESH (Pro-Active and Responsive facilitation by Interactive, Virtuous, Environmental, Single-window, Hi-tech) portal** for online EC applications — making it a digital-first compliance regime.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Comparison Matrix

Since this topic does not involve mathematical derivation in the classical sense, the **analytical "derivation"** here is the **comparative evolution framework** — i.e., how each clause of 2006 Notification was logically *derived* from a specific gap in the 1994 Notification.

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — From 1994 to 2006

Below is the exhaustive, step-by-step comparative evolution presented as aligned analytical statements:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Evolution Step 1: Categorization Logic} \\[4pt]
&\text{1994 Observation:} \;\; \text{All projects (small and large)} \xrightarrow{\text{single route}} \text{MoEF, Delhi} \\
&\text{Limitation Identified:} \;\; \text{Administrative overload, delay, no differential treatment.} \\
&\text{2006 Resolution:} \;\; \text{Projects} \xrightarrow{\text{Screening}} \{\text{Cat-A (Central)} \;\lor\; \text{Cat-B (State)}\} \\
&\text{Where:} \;\; \text{Cat-B} \xrightarrow{\text{Detailed Screening}} \{\text{B1 (Full EIA)} \;\lor\; \text{B2 (No full EIA)}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Evolution Step 2: Threshold Specification} \\[4pt]
&\text{1994 Observation:} \;\; \text{Threshold} = \text{undefined for most categories.} \\
&\text{Limitation Identified:} \;\; \text{Subjective interpretation, litigation, ambiguity.} \\
&\text{2006 Resolution:} \;\; \text{Threshold}_i = f(\text{Capacity}_i,\; \text{Area}_i,\; \text{Length}_i,\; \text{Investment}_i) \\
&\text{Where } i \in \{\text{Mining},\; \text{Thermal Power},\; \text{Highway},\; \text{Township},\; \text{Port},\ldots\}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Evolution Step 3: Public Hearing Proceduralization} \\[4pt]
&\text{1994 Observation:} \;\; \text{Public Hearing} = \text{Mandatory but procedurally vague.} \\
&\text{Limitation Identified:} \;\; \text{Inconsistent quality, manipulation, no public trust.} \\
&\text{2006 Resolution:} \;\; \text{PH} \xrightarrow{\text{SPCB}} \text{Conducted within 45 days of ToR} \\
&\hspace{2cm}\text{with 30-day prior public notice, verbatim record, district-level access.}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Evolution Step 4: Decentralization of Clearance} \\[4pt]
&\text{1994 Observation:} \;\; \text{Single central authority} = \text{Bottleneck.} \\
&\text{Limitation Identified:} \;\; \text{No local context assessment, project-specific delay.} \\
&\text{2006 Resolution:} \;\; \text{Cat-A} \xrightarrow{\text{EAC}} \text{MoEF\&CC (Central)} \\
&\hspace{2.5cm} \text{Cat-B} \xrightarrow{\text{SEAC}} \text{SEIAA (State/UT)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Evolution Step 5: Time-Bound Clearance} \\[4pt]
&\text{1994 Observation:} \;\; \text{No statutory timeline for EC grant.} \\
&\text{Limitation Identified:} \;\; \text{Indefinite delay, project cost inflation, investor uncertainty.} \\
&\text{2006 Resolution:} \;\; \text{Total EC Timeline} \leq \underbrace{60}_{\text{ToR}} + \underbrace{105}_{\text{Appraisal}} = 165 \text{ days (Cat-A)} \\
&\hspace{3cm} \leq 150 \text{ days (Cat-B with parallel processing).}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{Evolution Step 6: Validity and Monitoring} \\[4pt]
&\text{1994 Observation:} \;\; \text{EC once granted} = \text{perpetual, no monitoring.} \\
&\text{Limitation Identified:} \;\; \text{Ground-level non-compliance, post-EC environmental damage.} \\
&\text{2006 Resolution:} \;\; \text{Validity}_{EC} = \begin{cases} 5 \text{ years}, & \text{if River Valley / Hydel project} \\ 7 \text{ years}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \\
&\text{Monitoring:} \;\; \text{6-monthly compliance} \rightarrow \text{PARIVESH portal upload (post-2018).}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Detailed Category Expansion Table — Analytical Matrix

The 2006 Notification expanded scope significantly. The matrix below maps the **evolution of project coverage**:

| Sector | 1994 Notification | 2006 Notification (New / Enhanced) |
|---|---|---|
| **Industries** | 29 listed categories | Same 29 + 10 new (refineries, fertilizers, dyes, etc.) |
| **Mining** | Covered generically | Sector-specific threshold by **mining lease area (Ha)** |
| **Thermal Power** | Covered | Thresholds by **MW capacity** (< 500 MW = B; ≥ 500 MW = A) |
| **Highways** | Implicit | **Explicit** — length-based: ≥ 30 km in hills / ≥ 100 km in plains |
| **Airports** | Not covered | **Newly added** |
| **SEZs / Townships** | Not covered | **Newly added** |
| **Tourism (Large)** | Not covered | **Newly added** |
| **Petroleum Refining** | Partially covered | **Explicitly detailed** |

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Decision Logic — "Is EC Required for My Project?"

For a KTU-level numerical/decision question, the standard framework is:

$$\text{Step 1:} \;\; \text{Identify project type} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Map to Schedule (Item No.) of 2006 Notification}$$

$$\text{Step 2:} \;\; \text{Check threshold (Area / Capacity / Length / Investment)}$$

$$\text{Step 3:} \;\; \text{Check location sensitivity (Ecologically Sensitive Area list)}$$

$$\text{Step 4:} \;\; \text{If located in ESA} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Always Category A, regardless of size}$$

$$\text{Step 5:} \;\; \text{If not in ESA} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Apply threshold} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Determine A, B1, or B2}$$

$$\text{Step 6:} \;\; \text{Category A} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Apply to MoEF\&CC (EAC)}; \;\; \text{Category B} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Apply to SEIAA}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked *"To which authority will a 300 MW thermal power plant apply for EC?"* — the answer is **SEIAA (State)** because 300 MW < 500 MW threshold (Category B). A **600 MW thermal plant** = **MoEF&CC (Category A)**.

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example

> **Q:** *A company proposes a new open-cast coal mine of 75 hectares lease area in a non-ESA district. Under the 2006 Notification, determine:*
> *(a) Category of project*
> *(b) Authority for EC*
> *(c) Sub-steps required.*

**Solution:**

$$\text{Mining Lease Area} = 75 \text{ Ha} \quad (\text{Open-cast coal mine})$$

$$\text{As per Schedule, Item 1(a):} \;\; \geq 50 \text{ Ha but} < 150 \text{ Ha} \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Category B (State)}$$

$$\text{Since coal mining is a polluting industry} \;\Rightarrow\; \textbf{Category B1 (Full EIA required)}$$

$$\therefore \text{Clearance Authority} = \textbf{SEIAA (State Level)}$$

$$\text{Procedural Path:} \;\; \text{Screening (SEIAA)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{ToR (SEAC)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Public Hearing (SPCB)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{Appraisal (SEAC)} \;\rightarrow\; \text{EC (SEIAA)}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Architectural Flow — EIA Notification 1994 (Pre-Revision Regime)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Project Proponent Identifies Project Activity] --> B{Activity in 29 Listed Categories?}
    B -- No --> Z1[No EC Required]
    B -- Yes --> C[Submit EIA Report + EMP to MoEF Delhi]
    C --> D[Public Hearing - Procedure Vague]
    D --> E[Internal Appraisal by MoEF]
    E --> F{Environmental Clearance Granted?}
    F -- No --> Z2[Project Rejected]
    F -- Yes --> G[EC Granted - No Time Validity Defined]
    G --> H[No Mandatory Post-Clearance Monitoring]
```

> **Reading Guide:** The above diagram captures the **linear, single-window, vague-procedure** architecture of the 1994 Notification. Notice the absence of formal Screening and Scoping stages.

## 4.2 Architectural Flow — EIA Revised Notification 2006 (Current Regime)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Project Proponent Identifies Project Activity] --> Q{Activity in 39 Listed Categories?}
    Q -- No --> R1[No EC Required]
    Q -- Yes --> S[Stage 1 Screening by SEIAA or MoEFCC]
    S --> T{Is Project in Ecologically Sensitive Area?}
    T -- Yes --> U1[Category A - Central Route]
    T -- No --> V{Apply Sectoral Threshold Criteria}
    V --> W1[Category A if Threshold Exceeded]
    V --> W2[Category B if Below Threshold]
    W2 --> X[Stage 1b SEIAA Sub Screening]
    X --> Y1{B1 Full EIA Needed?}
    X --> Y2[B2 Brief Form Only No Full EIA]
    U1 --> Z[Stage 2 Scoping Terms of Reference within 60 days]
    W1 --> Z
    Y1 --> AA[Stage 3 EIA Report and Public Hearing within 45 days]
    Y2 --> BB[Stage 3 Brief EMP No Public Hearing]
    Z --> AA
    AA --> CC[Stage 4 Appraisal by EAC or SEAC within 105 days]
    BB --> CC
    CC --> DD{EC Decision}
    DD -- Granted --> EE[EC Validity 5 yr River Valley or 7 yr Other]
    EE --> FF[Post Clearance Monitoring 6 Monthly PARIVESH Portal]
    DD -- Rejected --> GG[Rejection Appeal to NGT]
```

> **Reading Guide:** The above diagram captures the **structured four-stage process** — Screening → Scoping → Public Hearing + EIA → Appraisal + EC Decision — with mandatory monitoring. Compare the **branching logic** with the linear flow of 1994.

## 4.3 Block-Level Comparative Architecture — 1994 vs 2006

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BLK_1994 [EIA NOTIFICATION 1994 ARCHITECTURE]
        direction TB
        B1A[Single Centralized Authority MoEF] --> B1B[Vague Thresholds]
        B1B --> B1C[No Screening Stage]
        B1C --> B1D[Informal Public Hearing]
        B1D --> B1E[No Time Bound Clearance]
        B1E --> B1F[No Post EC Monitoring]
    end
    subgraph BLK_2006 [EIA NOTIFICATION 2006 ARCHITECTURE]
        direction TB
        B2A[Dual Authority MoEFCC and SEIAA] --> B2B[Explicit Sectoral Thresholds]
        B2B --> B2C[Formal Screening Stage]
        B2C --> B2D[Standardized Public Hearing 45 days]
        B2D --> B2E[Time Bound 150 to 165 days]
        B2E --> B2F[Mandatory 6 Monthly PARIVESH Monitoring]
    end
    BLK_1994 -. Gap Identified .-> BLK_2006
```

> **Reading Guide:** This diagram visually demonstrates that the 2006 architecture is **architecturally derived from** the gaps of the 1994 architecture. Each block in the 2006 column is a **direct response** to a corresponding limitation in the 1994 column.

## 4.4 Hierarchical Categorization Schematic — 2006 Notification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[All Listed Projects in Schedule]
    H2{Is Project in ESA?}
    H2 -- Yes --> H3A[Category A - Always Central Level]
    H2 -- No --> H3B{Threshold Check}
    H3B -- Exceeds Threshold --> H4A[Category A - MoEFCC]
    H3B -- Below Threshold --> H4B[Category B - SEIAA Level]
    H4B --> H5{SEIAA Screening}
    H5 --> H6A[B1 - Full EIA + Public Hearing]
    H5 --> H6B[B2 - Form 1A Only - No Full EIA]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Use this schematic in answers to demonstrate the **decision-tree logic** of how a project is routed through the 2006 Notification framework.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## **PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 to 3 Marks Each)**

### **Q1. Define EIA Notification. State its legal basis.**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2022]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An **EIA Notification** is an executive legal instrument issued by the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF&CC)** under **Section 3(1) and 3(2)(v) of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**, that makes Environmental Impact Assessment and prior Environmental Clearance mandatory for specified categories of projects. *Legal Basis: EPA 1986, enacted in the aftermath of the Bhopal Gas Tragedy, 1984.*

---

### **Q2. Mention any four major limitations of the EIA Notification 1994.**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. Single central authority (MoEF) — administrative bottleneck, no state-level clearance.
2. Vague / undefined sectoral thresholds — ambiguity for many project sizes.
3. Public hearing procedure not standardized — inconsistent and open to manipulation.
4. No time-bound clearance — indefinite delays and no post-clearance monitoring mechanism.

---

---

## **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** *Explain in detail the structure, scope, and limitations of the EIA Notification 1994.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *Describe the major procedural innovations introduced by the EIA Revised Notification 2006.* **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Answer A(a) — Structure, Scope and Limitations of EIA Notification 1994 (7 Marks):**

The **EIA Notification 1994**, issued on **27 January 1994** by the Ministry of Environment & Forests (MoEF), was the **first comprehensive statutory instrument** mandating environmental clearance in India. Its structure, scope and limitations can be explained as follows:

**1. Legal Basis:** Issued under **Section 3(1) and 3(2)(v) of the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** — the parent umbrella Act passed after the Bhopal Tragedy.

**2. Scope:** Mandated EC for **29 categories of projects** including industries, mining, thermal power plants, ports, harbours, airports, and highways. Project proponents had to prepare an **EIA Report and Environmental Management Plan (EMP)** and submit it to MoEF, New Delhi.

**3. Public Hearing:** Introduced as a mandatory step before EC grant, but the notification did not prescribe a **standard procedure, time limit, or format**, leading to inconsistent implementation across states.

**4. Authority:** All clearances were processed centrally by **MoEF**, with no state-level EIA authority designated.

**5. Limitations:**
- Absence of clear **sectoral thresholds** (size/capacity cut-offs).
- **No screening or scoping** (Terms of Reference) mechanism.
- **No time-bound** clearance — projects could wait indefinitely.
- **No EC validity** period defined.
- **No post-clearance monitoring** obligation.
- Did not cover modern sectors like **SEZs, biotechnology parks, townships**.
- Single central authority created a **bottleneck** for thousands of small projects.

These limitations prompted the **comprehensive revision in 2006**.

---

### **Model Answer A(b) — Major Procedural Innovations of EIA Revised Notification 2006 (7 Marks):**

The **EIA Revised Notification 2006**, issued on **14 September 2006**, introduced the following landmark procedural innovations:

**1. Project Categorization — A and B System:**
- **Category A:** Large/impactful projects — cleared by **MoEF&CC** at Central level.
- **Category B:** Smaller/less impactful projects — cleared at **State level by SEIAA**, further sub-divided into **B1 (Full EIA + Public Hearing)** and **B2 (No full EIA, only Form-1A)**.

**2. Sector-Specific Thresholds:** Each of the **39 project categories** was assigned **quantitative thresholds** based on area (Ha), capacity (MW), length (km), or production volume — eliminating earlier ambiguity.

**3. Formal Screening and Scoping Stages:** A structured **Screening** stage determines A/B/B1/B2. **Scoping** issues formal **Terms of Reference (ToR)** within **60 days**, specifying the EIA study scope.

**4. Standardized Public Hearing:** Conducted by **SPCB within 45 days** of ToR, with **30 days' prior public notice**, verbatim record maintenance, and district-level accessibility.

**5. Time-Bound Clearance:** **ToR within 60 days**, **EC decision within 105 days** of EIA report submission — total **~150–165 days** statutory timeline.

**6. Decentralization:** Constitution of **SEIAA and SEAC** in every State/UT for local-context appraisal.

**7. EC Validity and Monitoring:** **5 years (River Valley)** / **7 years (others)** validity. **6-monthly compliance reports** mandatory via PARIVESH portal (post-2018).

**8. Expanded Scope:** Coverage extended from **29 to 39 categories** — including SEZs, townships, airports, large tourism projects, and biotechnology parks.

---

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** *Compare the EIA Notification 1994 and the EIA Revised Notification 2006 under the following heads:* **(i) Scope and coverage, (ii) Project categorization, (iii) Public hearing, (iv) Clearance authority, (v) Validity and monitoring.* **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** *With the help of a flowchart, describe the four-stage clearance process under the EIA Notification 2006. Mention the statutory time limit for each stage.* **(7 Marks)**

### **Model Answer B(a) — Comparative Table (7 Marks):**

| Head of Comparison | EIA Notification 1994 | EIA Revised Notification 2006 |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Scope and Coverage** | 29 categories of projects | Expanded to **39 categories**; SEZs, townships, airports added |
| **(ii) Project Categorization** | Single unified category; no sub-classification | **Category A** (Central) and **Category B** (State); B further split into **B1** (Full EIA) and **B2** (No full EIA) |
| **(iii) Public Hearing** | Mandatory but procedurally vague | Standardized — **SPCB**, **30 days prior notice**, **within 45 days of ToR** |
| **(iv) Clearance Authority** | Centralized — only **MoEF** | Decentralized — **MoEF&CC** for Cat-A, **SEIAA** for Cat-B |
| **(v) Validity and Monitoring** | Not defined | **5 yr (River Valley) / 7 yr (Other)**; **6-monthly compliance reports** via PARIVESH |

---

### **Model Answer B(b) — Four-Stage Clearance Process under EIA Notification 2006 (7 Marks):**

The 2006 Notification mandates a **structured four-stage process** for obtaining Environmental Clearance. The stages, with statutory time limits, are as follows:

**Stage 1 — Screening:**
The project proponent submits **Form-1 (or Form-1A for B2)** along with a pre-feasibility report. The authority (**MoEF&CC for Cat-A**, **SEIAA for Cat-B**) determines whether the project falls under Cat-A, Cat-B1, or Cat-B2 based on Schedule thresholds and ecologically sensitive area (ESA) location.

**Stage 2 — Scoping (Terms of Reference):**
For Cat-A and Cat-B1 projects, the **Expert Appraisal Committee (EAC) / State Expert Appraisal Committee (SEAC)** issues a formal **Terms of Reference (ToR)** specifying the scope of the EIA study. **Statutory limit: within 60 days** of application.

**Stage 3 — Public Hearing and EIA Report Preparation:**
The project proponent prepares the **EIA Report + Environmental Management Plan (EMP)** as per the ToR. **Public Hearing** is conducted by the **State Pollution Control Board (SPCB)** with 30 days' prior public notice. **Statutory limit: Public Hearing within 45 days** of ToR issuance.

**Stage 4 — Appraisal and EC Decision:**
The EIA Report and Public Hearing proceedings are appraised by **EAC (for Cat-A)** or **SEAC (for Cat-B)**. Based on the appraisal, the **MoEF&CC (for Cat-A)** or **SEIAA (for Cat-B)** grants or rejects the Environmental Clearance. **Statutory limit: EC decision within 105 days** of receipt of final EIA Report.

**Flowchart Summary:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Screening} \;\xrightarrow{\leq\, 60\,\text{days}}\; \text{Scoping (ToR)} \;\xrightarrow{\leq\, 45\,\text{days}}\; \text{Public Hearing + EIA} \;\xrightarrow{\leq\, 105\,\text{days}}\; \text{Appraisal + EC}}
$$

---

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MoEF with MoEF&CC:** The 1994 Notification was issued by **MoEF** (Forest was a separate Ministry then). The 2006 Notification was issued by **MoEF&CC** (after the merger). Students often lose 1 mark by writing the wrong ministry name.
>
> 2. **Wrong Section of EPA:** The correct legal basis is **Section 3(1) and 3(2)(v)** of EPA 1986. Do NOT write "Section 5" or "Section 19" — these belong to different Acts.
>
> 3. **Treating B1 and B2 as the same:** B1 needs full EIA + Public Hearing; B2 needs only a brief Form-1A and NO Public Hearing. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 4. **Forgetting the year difference in coverage:** 1994 had **29 categories**; 2006 has **39 categories**. Writing a generic "increased categories" without numbers loses 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Mixing up validity periods:** EC validity is **5 years for River Valley/Hydel projects** and **7 years for all other projects**. Reverse these and lose marks.
>
> 6. **Omitting Statutory Timeline:** Whenever you mention the 2006 Notification process, always quote the **60-day ToR, 45-day Public Hearing, 105-day EC decision** numbers. Examiners reward numerical specificity.
>
> 7. **Writing "EIA Act" instead of "EIA Notification":** There is NO Act called "EIA Act" in India. It is always a **Notification under the Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.

---

---

## **TOPIC RECAP — Rapid Revision Checklist**

- **EIA Notification 1994:** Issued on **27 January 1994** by **MoEF** under **Section 3(1) & 3(2)(v) of EPA, 1986**. **First** statutory mandate for EC. **29 categories**. Single central authority (MoEF). Vague thresholds. Vague public hearing procedure. No time-bound clearance. No EC validity. No post-clearance monitoring.

- **EIA Revised Notification 2006:** Issued on **14 September 2006** by **MoEF&CC**. **39 categories**. **Category A (Central — MoEF&CC) / Category B (State — SEIAA)** with **B1 (Full EIA) / B2 (Form-1A only)** sub-classification.

- **Sectoral Thresholds:** Explicit quantitative parameters — Ha for mining, MW for power, km for highways.

- **Four-Stage Process:** **Screening → Scoping (ToR ≤ 60 days) → Public Hearing (≤ 45 days of ToR) + EIA Report → Appraisal (≤ 105 days) → EC Decision.**

- **Statutory Bodies under 2006:** **EAC (Central)**, **SEAC (State)**, **SEIAA (State)**, **SPCB (Public Hearing)**.

- **EC Validity:** **5 years** for River Valley/Hydel; **7 years** for all others.

- **Public Hearing:** Conducted by **SPCB**, **30 days prior notice**, district-level access, verbatim record.

- **Post-Clearance Monitoring:** **6-monthly compliance reports** uploaded on **PARIVESH** portal.

- **Legal Chain:** **Constitution of India → EPA 1986 → EIA Notification 1994 → EIA Revised Notification 2006 → Subsequent Amendments (e.g., 2009, 2016, 2020 Draft).**

- **Key Buzzwords for Answer-Writing:** *Environment Protection Act 1986, MoEF&CC, Section 3, Category A/B, B1/B2, SEIAA, SEAC, ToR, PARIVESH, Ecologically Sensitive Area, 60-45-105 day timeline, Rio Earth Summit 1992, Bhopal Tragedy 1984.*

- **One-Line Comparative Punchline for Examiners:** *"The 2006 Notification transformed India's EIA regime from a centralized, vague, and discretionary system into a decentralized, threshold-based, time-bound, and monitorable clearance architecture."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Screening
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT632 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Screening

# 🟢 SCREENING IN EIA — STAGE 1 OF THE EIA PROCESS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Screening** is the *first and most fundamental stage* of the Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) process. It is a preliminary decision-making procedure used to determine **whether a proposed project requires an EIA**, and if so, **what level of detail (or "tier")** of environmental study is necessary.

In the official language of the KTU 2024 Scheme (aligned with the **EIA Notification, 2006** and its 2020 amendment under the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change, Government of India**):

> **Screening** is the process of examining a proposed project to decide whether it falls within the purview of the EIA Notification and consequently requires the preparation of an Environmental Impact Assessment Report (EIAR) and an Environmental Management Plan (EMP).

The screening stage produces one of **three possible decisions**:

| Decision Outcome | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Full EIA Required** | Project is large/sensitive; a complete EIA study must be conducted. |
| **Limited EIA / Rapid EIA** | Project is moderate; a smaller, focused study is sufficient. |
| **No EIA Required (Exemption)** | Project is small, benign, or falls outside the notification list. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Screening is essentially a *triage mechanism* — it sorts projects into categories of environmental risk so that regulatory authorities (SEIAA / SEAC at the State level, or MoEFCC at the Central level) can allocate their limited review resources to projects that pose the **greatest potential environmental threat**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Emergency Room

Imagine walking into a hospital emergency room. A **triage nurse** meets you at the door and asks a few rapid questions:

- *"Are you bleeding heavily?"*
- *"Are you having chest pain?"*
- *"Did you just fall off a ladder?"*

Based on these quick screening questions, the nurse classifies you as:
- 🔴 **Critical** → sent straight to the emergency doctor (equivalent to **Full EIA**)
- 🟡 **Moderate** → sent to a general physician (equivalent to **Limited EIA**)
- 🟢 **Minor** → given a band-aid and sent home (equivalent to **No EIA needed**)

**Screening in EIA works exactly the same way.** The regulatory authority acts as the "triage nurse," asking preliminary questions about the project's:
- **Size & Capacity** (e.g., area, production volume)
- **Type of Activity** (e.g., mining, thermal power, real estate)
- **Location** (e.g., near a forest, wetland, or protected area)
- **Potential Pollutant Emissions** (e.g., air, water, noise)

…and decides **how much environmental scrutiny** the project deserves.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Screening does *not* study the actual impacts of the project. It only decides **whether** an impact study is needed and **how detailed** that study should be. The actual impact study happens later, in the **Scoping** stage (Module 2) and **Impact Analysis** stage (Module 3).

---

### 1.3 Why Screening is Necessary — The "Why" Behind It

Environmental regulators worldwide handle **thousands of project proposals** every year. Conducting a full EIA for *every* project — even a small bakery or a 200 m² house — would be:

1. **Wasteful of expert time and public resources**
2. **Unnecessarily slow** for genuinely low-risk projects
3. **Burdening** for small-scale developers

Screening solves this by providing an **early, fast, and inexpensive** filter that separates the *wheat from the chaff*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric in KTU 2024 Module 2:** The screening process is governed by the **"Category A / Category B"** classification system in India. Category A projects (large, high-impact) require a full EIA and are cleared by the **Central Government (MoEFCC)**. Category B projects are further split into **B1** (requires EIA) and **B2** (does not require EIA) and are cleared by the **State Level Environment Impact Assessment Authority (SEIAA)**.

---

### 1.4 The Position of Screening in the EIA Cycle

Screening is **Stage 1** of the standard **8-stage EIA process** as taught in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

$$\text{EIA Process} = \underbrace{\text{Screening}}_{\text{Stage 1}} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Scoping}}_{\text{Stage 2}} \rightarrow \text{...} \rightarrow \underbrace{\text{Decision Making}}_{\text{Stage 8}}$$

It occurs *before* the proponent (project developer) invests heavily in consultants, baseline data collection, or public hearings.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🟢 SECTION 2 — DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD CRITERIA SHEET

## 2.1 The Two Pillars of Screening: *What* and *Where*

Screening decisions are made by evaluating two fundamental dimensions of the proposed project:

### **Pillar 1: Characteristics of the Project (the "WHAT")**
- **Project Type & Activity** — Is it mining, manufacturing, infrastructure, real estate, or tourism?
- **Scale / Size / Capacity** — What is the production volume, built-up area, or throughput?
- **Resource Consumption** — How much land, water, raw material, and energy will it use?
- **Emissions & Wastes** — Will it discharge pollutants into air, water, or land? Will it generate solid/hazardous waste?
- **Risk of Accidents** — Does it involve hazardous materials, explosives, or high-pressure systems?

### **Pillar 2: Characteristics of the Location (the "WHERE")**
- **Ecological Sensitivity** — Is the site near a National Park, Wildlife Sanctuary, Biosphere Reserve, or mangrove?
- **Proximity to Water Bodies** — Is it close to a river, lake, estuary, or coastal zone?
- **Cultural / Archaeological Heritage** — Are there protected monuments or tribal settlements nearby?
- **Population Density** — Will displacement or resettlement be required?
- **Existing Pollution Levels** — Is the area already a critically polluted industrial cluster?

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Conceptual Anchor:** Screening is **not a single test** — it is a **multi-criteria decision-making process**. The "score" the project receives across these two pillars determines the screening outcome.

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Screening Criterion

| Criterion | Engineering / Environmental Rationale |
|---|---|
| **Project Size** | Larger projects consume more resources and generate more waste → higher cumulative impact. |
| **Sensitive Location** | A project in a forest produces far more ecological damage than the same project in an industrial zone. |
| **Pollution Potential** | Projects emitting heavy metals, dioxins, or persistent organic pollutants need rigorous study. |
| **Reversibility of Impact** | Permanent damage (e.g., deforestation) demands more scrutiny than reversible damage (e.g., temporary noise). |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Methods of Screening — The "Cheat Sheet"

There are **four widely recognized methods** of screening used globally and examined in the KTU 2024 syllabus:

### **Method 1: List-Based (Positive / Negative Lists) — Most Common in India 🇮🇳**

This is the method used under the **EIA Notification, 2006 (as amended in 2020)**. The Government maintains an explicit list of:

- **Category A projects** (positive list) — *Always require a full EIA.* Examples: large thermal power plants (>500 MW), mining of major minerals, oil & gas exploration, etc.
- **Category B projects** (positive list) — *May or may not require EIA.* Examples: small industries, infrastructure projects below a threshold.
- **Exempted projects** (negative list) — *Do not require EIA.* Examples: small-scale cottage industries, individual houses.

| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Decision Rule** | Binary — Project is either on the list (→ EIA) or not (→ No EIA). |
| **Used By** | India (MoEFCC), most developing countries. |
| **Advantage** | Fast, transparent, legally binding. |
| **Disadvantage** | Inflexible — does not account for site-specific sensitivity. |

---

### **Method 2: Threshold-Based (Cut-off Values)**

Projects are screened based on **quantitative thresholds** such as:
- Built-up area (e.g., $>20{,}000 \text{ m}^2$)
- Capital investment
- Production capacity (e.g., $>100$ tonnes/day)
- Water consumption (e.g., $>1$ MLD)

| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Decision Rule** | If any threshold is exceeded → EIA required. |
| **Used By** | USA (NEPA thresholds), World Bank. |
| **Advantage** | Objective, unambiguous, easy to apply. |
| **Disadvantage** | May miss small projects with high local impact. |

---

### **Method 3: Matrix-Based (Leopold Matrix / Interaction Matrix)**

A two-dimensional matrix lists **project actions** on one axis and **environmental components** on the other. Each cell is scored for magnitude and importance.

| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Decision Rule** | Aggregate score above a threshold → EIA required. |
| **Used By** | Academic EIA studies, environmental consultants. |
| **Advantage** | Captures complex interactions, visual. |
| **Disadvantage** | Subjective scoring, time-consuming. |

---

### **Method 4: Expert Judgment / Case-by-Case**

A panel of experts reviews the project on its merits, considering unique circumstances.

| Parameter | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Decision Rule** | Consensus opinion of qualified experts. |
| **Used By** | European Union (for projects not on any list). |
| **Advantage** | Flexible, contextual. |
| **Disadvantage** | Slow, expensive, prone to bias. |

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula-Style Summary Table — Screening Decision Flow

Since EIA Screening is a **decision-logic** topic (not numerical), the "formula" is a **decision tree** rather than an equation. The KTU 2024 examiner expects students to know this flow:

$$
\boxed{
\text{Screening Outcome} = f(\text{Project Size, Project Type, Location Sensitivity, Pollution Potential})
}
$$

| Step | Question Asked | If YES | If NO |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Step 1** | Is the project on the Category A list? | Full EIA by MoEFCC | Go to Step 2 |
| **Step 2** | Is the project on the Category B list? | Go to Step 3 | **Exempted — No EIA** |
| **Step 3** | Is the project in an ecologically sensitive area? | Full EIA (B1 → treated as A) | Go to Step 4 |
| **Step 4** | Does the project cross any threshold (area, capacity, capital)? | Limited EIA | **Exempted — No EIA** |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Screening |
|---|---|
| **Civil Engineering** | Screening decides whether a new highway, bridge, or township must conduct baseline studies (water, air, noise, biodiversity). |
| **Mining Engineering** | Determines if an open-cast mine requires a full EIA or can proceed with a rapid environmental assessment. |
| **Chemical Engineering** | Factories handling hazardous chemicals (e.g., chlor-alkali, petrochemicals) almost always require full EIA after screening. |
| **Urban Planning** | Real estate projects above a built-up area threshold (e.g., $>20{,}000 \text{ m}^2$) trigger the EIA requirement. |
| **Renewable Energy** | Solar / wind farms in forested or tribal areas may be flagged for sensitive-location screening. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🟢 SECTION 3 — STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATION OF THE SCREENING PROCESS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 The Step-by-Step Screening Procedure (Detailed Walkthrough)

The screening process is a **structured, sequential decision-making algorithm**. Below is the complete step-by-step derivation, presented exactly as a KTU 14-mark answer would require.

---

### **Step 1 — Receipt of Project Proposal**

The project proponent (developer, company, or government agency) submits a **Form 1** (or its online equivalent on the **PARIVESH portal**) to the regulatory authority. Form 1 contains basic project information:

- Name and address of the project
- Type of activity
- Location with coordinates
- Proposed capacity / built-up area
- Raw material requirements
- Likely emissions and effluents

> **Logic:** Without a formal proposal, screening cannot begin. Form 1 acts as the *raw input* to the screening algorithm.

---

### **Step 2 — Initial Categorization (List Check)**

The regulatory authority consults the **EIA Notification Schedule** and asks:

$$
\text{Is the project activity listed in Schedule (Category A or B)?}
$$

- If **Category A** → Skip directly to **Full EIA**. *(No further screening needed.)*
- If **Category B** → Proceed to Step 3.
- If **Not Listed** → The project is **exempted**, but the authority may still conduct a discretionary review.

---

### **Step 3 — Location Sensitivity Assessment**

This is the **most critical sub-stage** of screening. The authority overlays the project location on official sensitivity maps:

| Map Layer | Source |
|---|---|
| National Parks & Wildlife Sanctuaries | **Wildlife Institute of India (WII)** |
| Critically Polluted Areas | **CPCB** |
| Coastal Regulation Zone (CRZ) | **MoEFCC / State Coastal Zone Management Authority** |
| Archaeologically Protected Monuments | **Archaeological Survey of India (ASI)** |
| Tribal / Scheduled Areas | **Ministry of Tribal Affairs** |
| Flood Plains & Wetlands | **Central Water Commission / State Wetland Authority** |

> **Logic:** A project that would be benign in an industrial zone may be devastating inside a tiger reserve. Therefore, *location can upgrade a project's category* — a Category B project in a sensitive area is often **re-categorized to Category A** and must undergo full EIA.

---

### **Step 4 — Threshold Verification**

For projects in Category B that are *not* in sensitive areas, the authority checks the **quantitative thresholds** specified in the notification. These are based on:

- **Capital investment** of the project
- **Built-up area** for construction projects
- **Production capacity** for industrial projects
- **Water consumption** in kilolitres per day (KLD)
- **Power requirement** in megawatts (MW)

$$
\text{EIA Required} = \begin{cases} \text{Yes} & \text{if metric} > \text{Threshold} \\ \text{No} & \text{if metric} \leq \text{Threshold} \end{cases}
$$

---

### **Step 5 — Public Consultation Trigger (if applicable)**

In India, even at the screening stage, certain projects may attract **public objections** (especially if local communities or NGOs raise concerns). If significant objections are received, the regulator may upgrade the project to a higher category.

---

### **Step 6 — Issuance of Screening Decision**

The final outcome is documented in **Form 1A** (or its online equivalent), which is a written communication to the project proponent stating one of the three decisions:

1. **Full EIA required** — proceed to Scoping (Module 2's next topic).
2. **Limited EIA / Rapid Environmental Assessment required.**
3. **No EIA required** — project can proceed with standard environmental clearances.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparing the Four Screening Methods

This matrix is a **high-yield item** for KTU exams, as comparative questions are very frequently asked:

| Evaluation Parameter | List-Based | Threshold-Based | Matrix-Based | Expert Judgment |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Speed of Decision** | ⚡ Very Fast | ⚡ Very Fast | 🐢 Slow | 🐢🐢 Very Slow |
| **Cost to Proponent** | 💲 Low | 💲 Low | 💲💲 Medium | 💲💲💲 High |
| **Objectivity** | ✅ High (binary) | ✅ High (numerical) | ⚠️ Medium (subjective scoring) | ❌ Low (opinion-based) |
| **Site Sensitivity Captured?** | ❌ No | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| **Legal Binding Nature** | ✅ Strong | ✅ Strong | ⚠️ Weak | ⚠️ Weak |
| **Used in India?** | ✅ Yes (primary) | ✅ Yes (secondary) | ⚠️ Rarely | ⚠️ Rarely |
| **Best For** | Routine projects | Standardized industries | Complex multi-impact projects | Unique / unprecedented projects |

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Worked Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Statement:**
> A real-estate developer proposes to construct a residential township with a total built-up area of **45,000 m²** on **200 acres of land** located **8 km away from the boundary of a National Park** in Kerala. The project will require **2.5 MLD of water** and will generate approximately **3.0 MLD of wastewater**. The capital investment is ₹850 crore. Apply the screening logic and determine the outcome.

### **Solution — Step by Step:**

**Step 1: List Check**
- Residential townships are listed under **Category B** in the EIA Notification 2020 (item 8(a) of the schedule, for projects with built-up area $>20{,}000 \text{ m}^2$ or investment $>$ ₹150 crore).
- Since built-up area = 45,000 m² (which is $>20{,}000 \text{ m}^2$) and investment = ₹850 crore (which is $>$ ₹150 crore), the project **qualifies as Category B**.

**Step 2: Location Sensitivity Check**
- The project is located **8 km from a National Park boundary**.
- Under the **EIA Notification**, a buffer of up to **10 km from the boundary of a National Park or Wildlife Sanctuary** is treated as an **eco-sensitive zone** for the purpose of project screening.
- Therefore, the project falls **within the eco-sensitive zone** of a protected area.

**Step 3: Consequence of Location Sensitivity**
- Per the **EIA Notification 2020, paragraph 7(ii)**: any Category B project located within **10 km of a National Park or Wildlife Sanctuary** shall be treated as a **Category A project** and must undergo a **full Environmental Impact Assessment**.
- (Some specific high-impact industries have a "No Go" condition where they cannot be located within 5 km of protected areas, but for real estate, the general EIA still applies.)

**Step 4: Threshold Check (irrelevant here because the project has already been upgraded to Category A)**
- Water consumption (2.5 MLD) and wastewater generation (3.0 MLD) are high but no longer matter for screening — the **full EIA is now mandatory** by virtue of location alone.

**Step 5: Final Screening Decision**

$$
\boxed{
\text{Screening Outcome: FULL EIA REQUIRED (Category A by virtue of eco-sensitive location)}
}
$$

> The project proponent must now:
> 1. Appoint a **NABET-accredited EIA Consultant** (Quality Council of India accreditation).
> 2. Conduct a **one-season baseline study** (post-monsoon preferred for Kerala).
> 3. Prepare an **EIA Report** and **Environmental Management Plan (EMP)**.
> 4. Hold a **Public Hearing** in the affected district.
> 5. Submit the EIA Report to **MoEFCC** (since it is now Category A) for clearance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this example is exam-relevant:** It demonstrates that **location sensitivity can override project size**. Even if the project were smaller, its proximity to a National Park would still trigger the full EIA. This is a very common twist in KTU question papers.

---

## 3.4 Decision Algorithm — The Screening Logic in Equation Form

Although EIA Screening is qualitative, we can express its decision logic mathematically:

$$
\text{Outcome} = f(S, L, T, P)
$$

Where:
- $S$ = Project Size / Scale metric
- $L$ = Location Sensitivity Index (binary: 0 or 1)
- $T$ = Threshold comparison result (boolean)
- $P$ = Pollution potential category (low / medium / high)

$$
\text{Outcome} = \begin{cases}
\text{Full EIA} & \text{if } (L = 1) \text{ OR } (S > S_{crit}) \text{ OR } (P = \text{high}) \\[4pt]
\text{Limited EIA} & \text{if } (L = 0) \text{ AND } (S_{crit2} < S \leq S_{crit}) \text{ AND } (P = \text{medium}) \\[4pt]
\text{No EIA} & \text{if } (L = 0) \text{ AND } (S \leq S_{crit2}) \text{ AND } (P = \text{low})
\end{cases}
$$

Where $S_{crit}$ is the higher threshold and $S_{crit2}$ is the lower threshold (for limited EIA).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
