5. Acts as a **complement to EIA** by managing ongoing operational environmental performance.

**Benefits:**

| Category | Benefit |
|----------|---------|
| Environmental | Reduced emissions, waste minimization, conservation of resources |
| Economic | Lower operating costs (energy, raw material savings), reduced insurance premiums |
| Legal | Demonstrated compliance, fewer violations |
| Social | Better community relations, improved employee morale |
| Strategic | Competitive advantage, easier market access, eligibility for green financing |
| Operational | Defined roles, fewer accidents, better emergency preparedness |

**Concluding Statement:** ISO 14001 transforms environmental management from a **cost burden** into a **strategic opportunity**, supporting **sustainable development** goals (SDGs).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**

**(a)** Describe the **stages involved in the implementation of ISO 14001** in a manufacturing industry. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **PDCA cycle** as applied to an EMS. How does it help in **continual improvement**? **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

### Part (a) — Stages of Implementation

| Stage | Activities |
|-------|-----------|
| **1. Initial Review & Gap Analysis** | Top management commitment, appoint EMS coordinator, identify current status vs. ISO requirements |
| **2. Environmental Policy** | Draft policy with pollution prevention, compliance, and continual improvement commitments |
| **3. Planning** | Identify aspects & impacts, determine significant aspects, register legal requirements, set SMART objectives |
| **4. Implementation & Operation** | Provide resources, train staff, document procedures, operational controls, emergency plans |
| **5. Checking & Corrective Action** | Monitor, internal audit, evaluation of compliance, address nonconformities |
| **6. Management Review & Certification** | Review EMS performance, identify improvements, undergo third-party audit for certification |
| **7. Continual Improvement** | Re-plan with new objectives, repeat the PDCA loop |

**Engineering Example (Civil/Construction):** A highway contractor identifies *dust generation* as a significant aspect, sets the objective *"Reduce dust emissions by 30% in 6 months"*, implements water-spraying controls, monitors PM10 levels, audits monthly, and improves continuously.

---

### Part (b) — PDCA Cycle in EMS

| PDCA Stage | ISO 14001 Activity | Outcome |
|------------|-------------------|---------|
| **Plan** | Identify aspects, risks, legal requirements; set objectives (Clauses 4, 6) | A documented environmental programme |
| **Do** | Implement controls, train workers, document information (Clauses 7, 8) | Operational compliance |
| **Check** | Monitor, audit, evaluate compliance (Clause 9) | Performance data & gaps |
| **Act** | Correct nonconformities, update EMS, improve (Clause 10) | Enhanced performance |

**Continual Improvement Link:**
After each cycle, objectives become more ambitious; significant aspects are re-evaluated; controls become stricter.

$$
\text{Performance}_{n+1} > \text{Performance}_n
$$

This iterative loop ensures that environmental management is **never static**, satisfying the **Clause 10 — Improvement** requirement of ISO 14001:2015.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner’s Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** ISO 14001 (Environmental) with ISO 9001 (Quality) or ISO 45001 (Safety) — students often mix clauses.
> 2. **Aspect vs. Impact** — examiners deduct **up to 2 marks** for confusing these in definition questions.
> 3. **PDCA = Plan-Do-Check-Act**, NOT "Plan-Do-Act-Check" — wrong order loses marks in flow diagrams.
> 4. **ISO 14001 is voluntary** — do not write it is "legally mandatory." It *helps* meet legal requirements but is not law itself.
> 5. Always mention **ISO 14001:2015** (latest version) — writing only "ISO 14001" without year is considered incomplete in 14-mark answers.
> 6. Skip the **10-clause table** in (a)-part answers at your own risk — it is the **single most expected structured content** in KTU ESE answers for this topic.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **ISO 14001:2015** is the **latest version** of the EMS standard, published by **ISO, Geneva**.
- It is a **voluntary, internationally accepted** framework.
- Built on the **PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act)** cycle.
- Uses the **Annex SL High-Level Structure** — 10 clauses.
- **Key Clauses to remember:** 4 (Context), 5 (Leadership), 6 (Planning), 8 (Operation), 9 (Evaluation), 10 (Improvement).
- **Aspect = Cause; Impact = Effect** — use the "A→E→E" trick.
- **Implementation Stages:** Gap Analysis → Policy → Planning → Implementation → Checking → Review → Continual Improvement.
- **Certification** requires a **third-party audit** by an accredited certification body.
- **Benefits span environmental, economic, legal, social, and strategic** domains.
- Integrates easily with **ISO 9001** and **ISO 45001** through the HLS.
- For KTU exams, always draw a **PDCA diagram** and a **10-clause table** — they guarantee high marks.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the 10 Clauses:** *"**S**o **N**o **T**alk, **C**ome **L**ets **P**lan **S**ome **O**perations, **E**valuate **I**mprovement"*
> (S=Scope, N=Normative Refs, T=Terms, C=Context, L=Leadership, P=Planning, S=Support, O=Operation, E=Evaluation, I=Improvement)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: EIA case studies (Indian) - a hydroelectric project, an agricultural production unit, food processing, fertilizer unit, irrigation project
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAGT632 - ENVIRONMENTAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** EIA case studies (Indian) - a hydroelectric project, an agricultural production unit, food processing, fertilizer unit, irrigation project

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an EIA Case Study?

**Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) Case Study** is a systematic, retrospective or prospective evaluation of the actual (or predicted) environmental, social, and economic consequences of a specific developmental project, conducted within a defined geographical boundary, using a standardized methodology (such as that prescribed by the **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEF&CC), Government of India**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> An EIA case study is the application of the EIA framework (screening → scoping → baseline study → impact prediction → mitigation → EMP → monitoring) to a real or proposed Indian project, with the objective of (a) validating the methodology, (b) understanding sector-specific impacts, and (c) formulating an implementable Environmental Management Plan.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, the Module 4 case studies are **not** abstract — they are expected to be grounded in actual Indian industrial and infrastructure sectors. The five sectors mandated by your syllabus are:

| S. No. | Sector | Typical Indian Example (KTU-Relevant) |
| :- | :- | :- |
| 1 | Hydroelectric Project | Nathpa Jhakri / Sardar Sarovar / Tehri |
| 2 | Agricultural Production Unit | Rice / Sugarcane / Tea estates |
| 3 | Food Processing Unit | Sugar mill, Dairy plant, Fruit processing |
| 4 | Fertilizer Unit | Urea / DAP / Ammonia complex |
| 5 | Irrigation Project | Indira Sagar / Bhakra / Nagarjuna Sagar |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Patient Diagnosis"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuition Box — "EIA = Medical Diagnosis of a Project"**
> Imagine a project is a **patient** about to undergo a major surgery (construction). Just as a doctor orders blood tests, X-rays, and ECG before surgery, an EIA:
> - **Screens** whether the patient needs the test at all (Category A or B).
> - **Scopes** which organs (air, water, soil, biodiversity) to examine.
> - **Establishes baseline** = the patient's current "normal" health parameters.
> - **Predicts impacts** = what could go wrong during/after surgery.
> - **Prescribes mitigation** = the medicines, lifestyle changes, and follow-ups.
> - **EMP** = the post-operative care plan.
> - **Monitoring** = regular check-ups after surgery.
>
> The **EIA case study** is essentially a **case file** of an already-diagnosed patient, used for teaching future doctors (engineers) what to look for.

## 1.3 Why Case Studies Matter in KTU Examinations

KTU examiners use case studies to test **higher-order thinking (Bloom Level 4 & 5 – Analyse & Evaluate)**. You are rarely asked to merely list impacts. Instead, you must:
- **Compare** impacts across sectors.
- **Justify** why a particular impact is "significant" for one sector but not another.
- **Recommend** specific mitigation measures with cost/benefit reasoning.
- **Critique** the EMP of a real project.

## 1.4 Key Cross-Sectoral Terminology (Foundational)

Before diving into each case, lock down these **bold** terms:

- **Project Proponent:** The entity (private company, PSU, government body) proposing the project (e.g., **NHPC Ltd.** for a hydro project).
- **Competent Authority:** The body that grants environmental clearance (in India: **MoEF&CC** at the central level, **State Environmental Impact Assessment Authority (SEIAA)** at the state level).
- **Category A Project:** Requires full EIA + public hearing (threshold capacities apply).
- **Category B Project:** Scoped EIA, may or may not require public hearing.
- **Terms of Reference (ToR):** The checklist issued by EAC/SEAC specifying what the EIA report must contain.
- **Environmental Management Plan (EMP):** The action plan with budget for mitigation, monitoring, and institutional responsibilities.
- **EAC / SEAC:** Expert Appraisal Committee (Central) / State Expert Appraisal Committee.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Tip:** When answering case study questions, always begin by identifying the **project category (A/B), the proponent, the location (state/district), and the statutory authority**. This alone fetches you 1–2 marks in a 14-mark question.

## 1.5 Common Attributes Across All Five Sectors

Although each sector is unique, the EIA framework remains identical. All five case studies share these **common impact domains**:

1. **Air environment** — SPM, RSPM, SO₂, NOₓ, CO.
2. **Water environment** — Surface water, groundwater, effluent quantity & quality.
3. **Land/Soil environment** — Land use change, contamination, erosion.
4. **Noise environment** — dB(A) levels at receptor sites.
5. **Biological environment** — Flora, fauna, endangered species, aquatic life.
6. **Socio-economic environment** — Displacement, livelihoods, cultural heritage.
7. **Occupational health & safety.**
8. **Solid & hazardous waste management.**

The **significance** and **magnitude** of each domain, however, vary dramatically by sector. This is the heart of the case study analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Unified EIA Methodology for Case Studies

Regardless of sector, every Indian EIA case study (post-2006 EIA Notification) follows this **8-stage lifecycle**:

| Stage | Activity | Output Document |
| :- | :- | :- |
| 1 | **Screening** | Decision on Category A or B |
| 2 | **Scoping** | Terms of Reference (ToR) |
| 3 | **Baseline Data Collection** | Baseline Status Report (one season × 2 or more) |
| 4 | **Impact Prediction** | Impact Matrix (Leopold / Matrix / Overlay) |
| 5 | **Assessment of Alternatives** | Site, technology, "No-Project" alternative |
| 6 | **Mitigation & EMP** | Environmental Management Plan with budget |
| 7 | **Public Hearing** | Public concerns record (for Category A & B1) |
| 8 | **Post-clearance Monitoring** | Half-yearly compliance reports |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In a 14-mark case study answer, you must touch upon **at least Stages 1, 3, 4, 6, and 7** to score above 10/14.

## 2.2 Sector 1 — Hydroelectric Project Case Study

### 2.2.1 Project Anatomy

A hydroelectric project (HEP) typically involves:
- A **dam/barrage/weir** across a river.
- An **intake structure** and **headrace tunnel/channel**.
- A **penstock** carrying water to the powerhouse.
- A **powerhouse** with turbines and generators.
- A **tailrace** returning water to the river.
- A **reservoir** (large or run-of-the-river).
- **Transmission lines** and substations.
- **Access roads, township, and construction camps.**

### 2.2.2 Baseline Environment Considerations

| Parameter | Why Critical for HEP |
| :- | :- |
| **Hydrology** | Flow regime, lean season discharge, flood peaks |
| **Aquatic ecology** | Fish migration routes, breeding grounds, endemic species |
| **Geology/Seismicity** | Reservoir-induced seismicity, slope stability |
| **Forest cover** | Submergence area, muck disposal sites |
| **Downstream ecology** | Tailrace effects on river continuum |
| **Sediment transport** | Reservoir siltation, downstream bed erosion |

### 2.2.3 Key Environmental Impacts

1. **Submergence of land** — agricultural fields, forest, settlements.
2. **Displacement and rehabilitation** of project-affected families (PAFs).
3. **Loss of forest & biodiversity** — reservoir area, muck disposal.
4. **Alteration of river flow regime** — downstream ecological disruption.
5. **Reservoir-induced seismicity** (RIS) — triggered earthquakes.
6. **Greenhouse gas emissions from reservoir** — CH₄ from decomposing biomass (especially in tropical reservoirs).
7. **Impact on fish & aquatic life** — blocked migration, turbine mortality.
8. **Water-logging and salinization** in upstream areas.
9. **Sediment trapping** in reservoir, leading to downstream riverbed degradation.
10. **Construction phase impacts** — blasting, muck disposal, worker camps.

### 2.2.4 Mitigation & EMP Highlights

- **Catchment Area Treatment (CAT) Plan** to control siltation.
- **Compensatory Afforestation** (CA) — typically at 1:1 or 2:1 ratio.
- **Resettlement & Rehabilitation (R&R) Plan** per National R&R Policy, 2007 (or 2013 Right to Fair Compensation and Transparency in Land Acquisition, Rehabilitation and Resettlement Act, RFCTLARR).
- **Fish ladder/pass** at the barrage.
- **Environmental flow (e-flow) release** — minimum downstream discharge.
- **Greenhouse gas mitigation** — pre-impoundment biomass clearing.
- **Muck disposal plan** with engineered dumpsites.
- **Reservoir rim treatment** to prevent landslides.

### 2.2.5 Reference Case — Nathpa Jhakri Hydroelectric Project (1500 MW, Himachal Pradesh)

| Attribute | Value |
| :- | :- |
| Proponent | Satluj Jal Vidyut Nigam Ltd. (SJVN) |
| Location | Kinnaur & Shimla districts, HP |
| River | Sutlej |
| Type | Run-of-the-river with small reservoir |
| Forest land diverted | ~1,143 ha |
| PAFs displaced | ~2,500 families |
| Specific concerns | Sediment-laden Sutlej (one of highest sediment-yielding rivers), muck disposal in fragile Himalayan geology, fish (Tor putitora) conservation |

## 2.3 Sector 2 — Agricultural Production Unit Case Study

### 2.3.1 Project Anatomy

Modern agricultural production units may include:
- **Land preparation** (clearing, leveling).
- **Irrigation infrastructure** (canals, wells, drip/sprinkler).
- **Crop cultivation** (monoculture or multi-crop).
- **Storage** (silos, godowns, cold storage).
- **Processing** (on-site mills, sorters, packers).
- **Worker housing and transport.**

### 2.3.2 Key Environmental Impacts

1. **Land-use change** — deforestation for farm expansion.
2. **Soil degradation** — erosion, salinization, waterlogging, desertification.
3. **Water demand & depletion** — over-extraction of groundwater.
4. **Agro-chemical contamination** — fertilizers, pesticides in soil and water.
5. **Eutrophication** — nutrient runoff into water bodies.
6. **Loss of agrobiodiversity** — shift from local varieties to hybrids/GMOs.
7. **Greenhouse gas emissions** — CH₄ from paddy, N₂O from fertilizers, CO₂ from machinery.
8. **Pest resurgence & resistance.**
9. **Impact on pollinators.**
10. **Health impacts on farm workers** (pesticide exposure).

### 2.3.3 Mitigation & EMP Highlights

- **Integrated Pest Management (IPM).**
- **Integrated Nutrient Management (INM).**
- **Precision farming & drip irrigation.**
- **Crop rotation & cover crops.**
- **Buffer zones** around water bodies.
- **Organic farming** transitions.
- **Rainwater harvesting** for groundwater recharge.
- **Soil & water testing labs** for farmer use.

### 2.3.4 Reference Case — Indira Gandhi Nahar Pariyojana (IGNP) command area development, Rajasthan

| Attribute | Value |
| :- | :- |
| Type | Large-scale irrigated agricultural development in Thar Desert |
| Key concerns | Waterlogging, secondary salinization, soil degradation in sandy terrain |
| Mitigation | Subsurface drainage, agro-forestry (Shelterbelt Plantation), salt-tolerant crop varieties |

## 2.4 Sector 3 — Food Processing Unit Case Study

### 2.4.1 Project Anatomy

A typical food processing unit (e.g., a sugar mill, dairy, fruit processing, brewery, meat processing) has:
- **Raw material receiving & storage** (sugarcane yard, milk reception dock).
- **Processing hall** — crushers, evaporators, fermenters, etc.
- **Boilers** (bagasse/coal/fuel-oil fired) for steam.
- **Effluent Treatment Plant (ETP)** — wastewater handling.
- **Product storage & dispatch** (warehouses, cold rooms).
- **Utility section** — DG sets, water treatment, refrigeration.
- **Solid waste handling** — press mud, bagasse, fruit peel, etc.

### 2.4.2 Key Environmental Impacts

1. **High BOD/COD effluent** — biodegradable organic load.
2. **Suspended solids & colour** in wastewater.
3. **High water consumption.**
4. **Air emissions from boiler** — SPM, SO₂, NOₓ.
5. **Odour nuisance** (especially meat, fish, fermentation).
6. **Solid waste** — press mud, bagasse, fruit/vegetable waste, sludge.
7. **Noise** from crushers, DG sets.
8. **Pest attraction** (flies, rodents) — public health risk.
9. **Land contamination** from ETP sludge if not disposed scientifically.
10. **Occupational health** — noise, heat, dust, biological hazards.

### 2.4.3 Mitigation & EMP Highlights

- **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)** — recycling treated water back to process.
- **Biogas generation** from press mud / food waste via anaerobic digestion.
- **Bagasse-based cogeneration** for renewable energy in sugar mills.
- **Composting** of organic solid waste.
- **Condensate recovery** to reduce freshwater intake.
- **Online effluent quality monitoring** connected to CPCB/SPCB servers.
- **HACCP / ISO 22000** for food safety and waste segregation at source.

### 2.4.4 Reference Case — Sugar Mill (e.g., a typical 5000 TCD mill, Maharashtra)

| Parameter | Typical Value |
| :- | :- |
| Water consumption | ~1500–2000 litres per tonne of cane crushed |
| Effluent quantity | ~500–1000 L/TC |
| Effluent BOD | ~2000–3000 mg/L (spent wash) |
| Solid waste | Press mud (~3–4% of cane), bagasse (~30% of cane) |
| Key concern | Spent wash (from distillation) — high BOD & dark colour |

## 2.5 Sector 4 — Fertilizer Unit Case Study

### 2.5.1 Project Anatomy

A nitrogenous fertilizer plant (e.g., urea from ammonia) involves:
- **Ammonia plant** — natural gas/naptha feedstock, steam reforming, CO₂ shift, ammonia synthesis.
- **Urea plant** — CO₂ + NH₃ → ammonium carbamate → urea prilling/granulation.
- **Utility section** — captive power, cooling towers, DM plant.
- **Storage** — ammonia tank farm, bagging plant.
- **Effluent Treatment Plant.**
- **Hazardous waste incinerator / secure landfill.**

For **phosphatic fertilizers** (SSP, DAP, MAP):
- **Rock phosphate** crushing, acidulation with H₂SO₄, granulation.
- **Gypsum** generation (in SSP).
- **Fluorine & silica dust** emissions.
- **Heavy metal contamination** (Cd, Pb, As in rock phosphate).

### 2.5.2 Key Environmental Impacts

1. **Air emissions** — NH₃, NOₓ, SO₂, CO₂, fluorides, particulates.
2. **High water consumption** — cooling, process, steam.
3. **Liquid effluent** — ammonia, urea, oil, suspended solids.
4. **Solid waste** — catalyst sludge, gypsum, slag, spent adsorbents.
5. **Hazardous waste** — spent mercury (in old chlor-alkali-coupled plants), arsenic sludge.
6. **Greenhouse gas footprint** — CO₂ from reforming, N₂O from nitric acid.
7. **Odour** — ammonia, amine smell.
8. **Heavy metal release** — Cd in phosphate fertilizer.
9. **Major accident hazards (MAH)** — ammonia, chlorine, H₂SO₄ storage.
10. **Soil & groundwater contamination** from effluent disposal.

### 2.5.3 Mitigation & EMP Highlights

- **Process modifications** — low-energy ammonia plants, CO₂ stripping.
- **N₂O abatement** — catalytic reduction in nitric acid plants.
- **Ammonia recovery from effluent** — stripping columns.
- **Fluoride control** — scrubbers in phosphatic plants.
- **Closed-loop cooling & DM water regeneration.**
- **On-site Emergency Plan (OEP)** and **Off-site Emergency Plan** for ammonia.
- **Hazard & Operability (HAZOP) Study.**
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA).**
- **CNG/natural gas as feedstock** to reduce emissions.

### 2.5.4 Reference Case — Krishak Bharati Cooperative (KRIBHCO) — Hazira, Gujarat

| Parameter | Value |
| :- | :- |
| Capacity | 1.95 million tonnes urea/year (one of world's largest single-stream) |
| Feedstock | Natural gas from South Bassein field |
| Key concerns | Fugitive ammonia, CO₂ emissions, cooling tower drift, hazardous catalyst disposal |
| EMP highlights | ISO 14001 certification, ambient air monitoring stations, online stack analyzers, rainwater harvesting |

## 2.6 Sector 5 — Irrigation Project Case Study

### 2.6.1 Project Anatomy

A major irrigation project (e.g., a dam + canal network) consists of:
- **Dam/Barrage** on a river.
- **Main canal, branch canals, distributaries, minors.**
- **Field channels** to farms.
- **Drainage system** (surface & subsurface).
- **Command area development** (CAD) works.
- **Colonies & offices.**

### 2.6.2 Key Environmental Impacts

1. **Submergence & displacement** (dam component).
2. **Waterlogging** in command area due to over-irrigation and poor drainage.
3. **Secondary salinization & sodicity** — rising water table dissolves salts.
4. **Groundwater depletion or contamination** (depending on canal lining).
5. **Vector-borne diseases** — malaria, filariasis, schistosomiasis from stagnant water.
6. **Biodiversity loss** — wetlands, riparian habitat.
7. **Downstream riverine impacts** — reduced flow, sediment starvation, delta degradation.
8. **Soil erosion in command area** from excessive irrigation.
9. **Siltation of reservoir** reducing storage capacity.
10. **Social impacts** — displacement, disruption of fisheries, cultural sites.

### 2.6.3 Mitigation & EMP Highlights

- **Conjunctive use** of surface & groundwater.
- **Lined canals in permeable zones** to prevent waterlogging.
- **Subsurface drainage systems** in waterlogged pockets.
- **On-farm water management** — land leveling, border strips, drip/sprinkler.
- **Command Area Development (CAD) Programme.**
- **Catchment Area Treatment** for silt control.
- **Health surveillance** for vector-borne diseases.
- **Environmental flow** maintenance downstream of dam.
- **Fishery development** in reservoir.

### 2.6.4 Reference Case — Indira Sagar Project (ISP), Madhya Pradesh

| Parameter | Value |
| :- | :- |
| Capacity | 1000 MW hydro + 1.23 MCM irrigation |
| Submergence area | ~91,000 ha (large) |
| Villages affected | ~249 |
| PAFs | ~1.1 lakh |
| Key concerns | Forest submergence in Satpura hills, large-scale R&R, downstream Narmada valley environment |
| EMP highlights | Joint Forest Management (JFM), compensatory afforestation, R&R colonies, downstream flow regulation |

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

Although EIA is qualitative, several **quantitative formulas** are tested in the KTU exam. These are derived from environmental engineering.

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Use Case | Units |
| :- | :- | :- | :- |
| **BOD Load** | $L = Q \times BOD$ | Total organic load from effluent | kg/day |
| **BOD₅ First-order Kinetics** | $BOD_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right)$ | Oxygen demand in receiving water | mg/L |
| **Dilution factor** | $DF = \dfrac{Q_{river}}{Q_{effluent}}$ | Mixing of effluent in stream | dimensionless |
| **Streeter–Phelps DO Sag** | $D_t = \dfrac{k_d \cdot L_0}{k_r - k_d}\left(e^{-k_d t} - e^{-k_r t}\right) + D_0 \cdot e^{-k_r t}$ | Critical DO deficit downstream | mg/L |
| **Carbon footprint (CO₂-eq)** | $CF = \sum_i GWP_i \times m_i$ | GHG emissions inventory | tCO₂e/yr |
| **Noise Attenuation (point source)** | $L_{p}(r) = L_{p}(r_0) - 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{r}{r_0}\right)$ | Far-field noise | dB |
| **Noise Attenuation (line source — road traffic)** | $L_{p}(r) = L_{p}(r_0) - 10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{r}{r_0}\right)$ | Traffic noise | dB |
| **Stack Plume Rise (Briggs)** | $\Delta h = \dfrac{1.6 \cdot v_s \cdot d \cdot (F)^{1/3}}{u}$ (neutral) | Effective stack height | m |
| **Buoyancy flux** | $F = g \cdot v_s \cdot d^2 \cdot \dfrac{\Delta T}{T_s}$ | Plume rise input | m⁴/s³ |
| **Catchment Sediment Yield** | $SY = C \cdot A^n$ | Reservoir siltation estimate | t/yr |
| **Land Requirement per MW (HEP)** | $LR = \dfrac{A_{sub}}{P}$ | Footprint of hydro project | ha/MW |
| **Water Footprint of Crop** | $WF = ET_{crop} \times 10\ \text{m}^3/\text{ha}$ per crop | Agricultural water demand | m³/season |
| **Standard Power Formula (HEP)** | $P = \eta \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H$ | Installed capacity | W (with $\eta$ = 0.85, $\rho$ = 1000 kg/m³, $g$ = 9.81 m/s²) |
| **Efficiency of ETP** | $\eta_{ETP} = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$ | Treatment plant performance | % |
| **Fugitive Emission Factor (NH₃)** | $E_f = \phi \times E_{total}$ | Leakage inventory | kg/hr |
| **R&R Cost Ratio (rule of thumb)** | $R\&R_{cost} = 0.4 \times \text{Project Cost}$ | Budgetary check | – |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap Alert:** When asked "calculate the dilution factor," do not confuse $Q_{river}$ with total flow. Use **lean season (90% dependable) flow**, not average. Examiners deduct marks for this.

## 2.8 Comparative Sectoral Impact Matrix (KTU-Favorite Table)

This table is the **single most high-yield chart** for Module 4. Memorize the impact significance (Hi / Med / Lo).

| Impact Domain | Hydro | Agriculture | Food Processing | Fertilizer | Irrigation |
| :- | :- | :- | :- | :- | :- |
| Air (SPM/SO₂) | Med | Low | Med-Hi | **Hi** | Low |
| Air (GHG) | Med (CH₄ reservoir) | **Hi** (N₂O, CH₄) | Low | **Hi** (CO₂, N₂O) | Med |
| Water (effluent vol) | Low | Med (runoff) | **Hi** | Med | Low |
| Water (quality) | Med (DO, temp) | **Hi** (pesticides) | **Hi** (BOD) | **Hi** (NH₃, F) | Med (salinity) |
| Land use change | **Hi** (submergence) | **Hi** (deforestation) | Med | Med | **Hi** (submergence) |
| Biodiversity | **Hi** (aquatic) | Med (agro-eco) | Low | Low | Med |
| Displacement/R&R | **Hi** | Low-Med | Low | Low | **Hi** |
| Solid/Haz waste | Med (muck) | Med (containers) | Med (sludge) | **Hi** (catalyst) | Med (silt) |
| Major accident hazard | Low (dambreak) | Low (pesticides) | Low (boiler) | **Hi** (NH₃) | Low (dambreak) |
| Public health | Med (vector) | Med (pesticide) | Med (zoonotic) | Med (toxic) | **Hi** (vector) |

**Bolded "Hi" cells** are the dominant impact per sector — KTU questions often ask: *"Identify the most significant environmental impact of a fertilizer unit and justify."*

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Numerical — Hydroelectric Installed Capacity (KTU Favorite)

**Problem:** A run-of-the-river hydro project on the Sutlej has a design discharge of $Q = 240\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ and a gross head of $H_g = 75\ \text{m}$. Head loss in the water conductor system is $h_L = 4.5\ \text{m}$. Turbine-generator efficiency is $\eta = 0.86$. Calculate the **installed capacity (MW)** and the **annual energy generation (GWh)** at a **plant load factor (PLF)** of 0.55.

### Step 1 — Compute Net Head

The **net head** is the gross head minus all hydraulic losses in the penstock, trash racks, and tailrace:

$$
H_{net} = H_g - h_L
$$

Substitute the values:

$$
H_{net} = 75 - 4.5 = 70.5\ \text{m}
$$

The loss $h_L$ accounts for friction in the headrace tunnel, penstock friction, trash rack losses, and tailrace losses. In a 14-mark question, **always show this subtraction explicitly** — examiners mark it.

### Step 2 — Apply the Standard Power Formula

$$
P = \eta \cdot \rho \cdot g \cdot Q \cdot H_{net}
$$

Substitute (with **$\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$**, **$g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$**):

$$
P = 0.86 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 240 \times 70.5
$$

Compute step-by-step:

$$
9.81 \times 240 = 2354.4
$$

$$
2354.4 \times 70.5 = 166{,}985.2
$$

$$
166{,}985.2 \times 0.86 = 143{,}607.27\ \text{W (mech+elec)}
$$

$$
P = 143{,}607.27\ \text{kW} = 143.61\ \text{MW}
$$

### Step 3 — Annual Energy Generation

Total hours in a year: $8760\ \text{h}$.

$$
E_{annual} = P \times PLF \times 8760
$$

$$
E_{annual} = 143.61 \times 10^6\ \text{W} \times 0.55 \times 8760\ \text{h}
$$

Compute:

$$
E_{annual} = 143.61 \times 0.55 \times 8760\ \text{MWh}
$$

$$
143.61 \times 0.55 = 78.99\ \text{MW (effective)}
$$

$$
78.99 \times 8760 = 691{,}948.4\ \text{MWh} = 691.95\ \text{GWh}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify with Environmental Capacity (Conceptual)

Compare $E_{annual}$ with the regional power demand. For an EIA, the **specific emissions displaced** = $0.82\ \text{tCO}_2/\text{MWh}$ for the grid average (CEA 2023). Therefore:

$$
\text{CO}_2\ \text{avoided} = 691{,}948 \times 0.82 = 567{,}397.36\ \text{tCO}_2/\text{year}
$$

This is what gets reported in the **Greenhouse Gas Impact** section of the EIA report.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's note:** Showing the "greenhouse gas avoided" is the **single best way** to convert a dry engineering calculation into an EIA-aware answer.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Streeter–Phelps DO Sag in a River Receiving Sugar Mill Effluent

**Problem:** A sugar mill discharges $Q_{eff} = 0.05\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ of spent wash with $BOD_5 = 2500\ \text{mg/L}$ into a river of flow $Q_r = 2.5\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$ and DO $= 7.5\ \text{mg/L}$ (saturation $DO_{sat} = 8.0\ \text{mg/L}$). Given deoxygenation constant $k_d = 0.32\ \text{/day}$, reaeration constant $k_r = 0.52\ \text{/day}$, and river velocity $v = 0.45\ \text{m/s}$, find the **critical distance** at which DO is minimum.

### Step 1 — Mix the Effluent with the River (Mass Balance)

**Dilution factor:**

$$
DF = \dfrac{Q_r}{Q_r + Q_{eff}} = \dfrac{2.5}{2.5 + 0.05} = 0.9804
$$

**Mixed BOD₅:**

$$
L_0 = BOD_5 \times \dfrac{Q_{eff}}{Q_r + Q_{eff}} = 2500 \times \dfrac{0.05}{2.55} = 49.02\ \text{mg/L}
$$

**Mixed DO:**

$$
DO_{mix} = DF \cdot DO_{river} + (1 - DF) \cdot DO_{eff}
$$

Assuming effluent $DO \approx 0$:

$$
DO_{mix} \approx 0.9804 \times 7.5 = 7.353\ \text{mg/L}
$$

**Initial DO deficit:**

$$
D_0 = DO_{sat} - DO_{mix} = 8.0 - 7.353 = 0.647\ \text{mg/L}
$$

### Step 2 — Critical Time (Streeter–Phelps)

The **critical time** $t_c$ at which DO is minimum is:

$$
t_c = \dfrac{1}{k_r - k_d} \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{k_r}{k_d} \cdot \left[1 - D_0 \cdot \dfrac{(k_r - k_d)}{k_d \cdot L_0}\right]\right)
$$

Compute the inner term first:

$$
\dfrac{k_r}{k_d} = \dfrac{0.52}{0.32} = 1.625
$$

$$
\dfrac{(k_r - k_d)}{k_d \cdot L_0} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.32 \times 49.02} = \dfrac{0.20}{15.69} = 0.01275
$$

$$
D_0 \cdot 0.01275 = 0.647 \times 0.01275 = 0.00825
$$

$$
1 - 0.00825 = 0.99175
$$

$$
1.625 \times 0.99175 = 1.6116
$$

$$
\ln(1.6116) = 0.4772
$$

$$
t_c = \dfrac{0.4772}{0.20} = 2.386\ \text{days}
$$

### Step 3 — Critical Distance

$$
x_c = v \cdot t_c \times 86400\ \text{(s/day)} = 0.45 \times 2.386 \times 86400
$$

$$
x_c = 92{,}812.8\ \text{m} \approx 92.8\ \text{km}
$$

### Step 4 — Critical DO Deficit

The critical deficit formula:

$$
D_c = \dfrac{k_d}{k_r} \cdot L_0 \cdot e^{-k_d t_c}
$$

Compute:

$$
\dfrac{k_d}{k_r} = \dfrac{0.32}{0.52} = 0.6154
$$

$$
e^{-0.32 \times 2.386} = e^{-0.7635} = 0.4661
$$

$$
D_c = 0.6154 \times 49.02 \times 0.4661 = 14.05\ \text{mg/L}
$$

**But** $D_c$ cannot exceed $DO_{sat}$. This indicates **anaerobic conditions** — the river will go septic at the critical point unless the mill pre-treats.

### Step 5 — EIA Interpretation

In the EIA report, the conclusion is:
> *"Sugar mill effluent, even after 98% dilution, will cause complete deoxygenation of the receiving river within 2.4 days of travel (~93 km). Strict Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD) and pre-treatment to BOD < 100 mg/L is mandated before discharge."*

This is the **textbook reasoning** for a sugar mill EMP.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Mitigation per Sector

This is the **decision matrix** that KTU examiners test under "Justify your recommendation."

| Project Type | Dominant Impact | Mandatory Mitigation | Statutory Reference |
| :- | :- | :- | :- |
| **Hydroelectric** | Submergence, displacement, biodiversity | R&R plan, CAT plan, e-flow release, fish pass | RFCTLARR Act 2013, Forest Conservation Act 1980 |
| **Agriculture** | Soil degradation, agro-chemicals | IPM/INM, drip irrigation, buffer zones | Insecticides Act 1968, Water Act 1974 |
| **Food Processing** | High BOD effluent, solid waste | ETP + biogas, composting, ZLD | Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, MSW Rules 2016 |
| **Fertilizer** | Air emissions, major accident hazard | N₂O abatement, ammonia recovery, OSEP | MSIHC Rules 1989, Manufacture/Storage Rules |
| **Irrigation** | Waterlogging, salinization, displacement | Drainage, lining, CAD, R&R | RFCTLARR Act 2013, Water Act 1974 |

## 3.4 Cross-Sector Analytical Reasoning (Bloom Level 4–5)

KTU often asks: *"Compare the environmental impacts of a hydroelectric project and an irrigation project."*

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

1. **Both** involve submergence, displacement, and downstream flow alteration.
2. **Hydro project** has a *single-point* large dam; **Irrigation** has a *distributed network* of canals and command area.
3. **Hydro** displaces forest, settlements in the reservoir; **Irrigation** primarily affects command-area agriculture (waterlogging/salinity).
4. **Hydro** has reservoir-induced GHG and fish migration issues; **Irrigation** has vector-borne disease and siltation issues.
5. **Hydro** generates clean electricity; **Irrigation** enables agriculture but with fertilizer/pesticide load.

> [!TIP]
> **Conclusion to write:** "While both projects share a hydraulic foundation, their *spatial footprint* and *temporal impacts* differ markedly — hydro's impacts are concentrated and episodic (construction); irrigation's are diffuse and chronic (operational)."

## 3.5 Mathematical Justification of Environmental Flow (E-flow)

**Problem:** A river has a mean annual flow of $Q_{mean} = 500\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. The project requires $Q_{diversion} = 220\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. What is the **minimum environmental flow** required to maintain the river's ecological functions, using the **Tennant (Montana) method**?

The **Tennant method** prescribes the **percentage of mean flow** that must remain in the river:

| Condition | % of Mean Flow |
| :- | :- |
| Flushing flow (channel maintenance) | 200% (intermittent) |
| Optimum habitat | 60–100% |
| Outstanding habitat | 40–60% |
| Good habitat | 30% |
| Minimum / poor | 10% |
| Severe degradation | < 10% |

For a typical run-of-the-river HEP, a **30% of mean flow** is mandated as the **minimum e-flow**, with a **60% assured flow** as the **lean season e-flow**.

### Calculation

$$
Q_{e-flow, min} = 0.30 \times 500 = 150\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

$$
Q_{e-flow, lean} = 0.60 \times 500 = 300\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}
$$

### Check Feasibility

Diversion = 220 m³/s. After diversion, downstream flow = $500 - 220 = 280\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$. Since 280 > 150 and > 300 in dry season with reservoir support, the project is feasible. **Without** reservoir, lean season e-flow of 300 m³/s is **not** met, indicating the **need for reservoir storage or reduced diversion**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Many students compute the e-flow as **after-diversion flow** ($280\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$) without applying the Tennant percentage. The correct procedure is to **first compute the e-flow requirement**, **then** check if the post-diversion flow meets it.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*
**"Differentiate between Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) and Environmental Management Plan (EMP) with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | EIA | EMP |
| :- | :- | :- |
| **Purpose** | Predictive study to identify likely impacts | Action plan to manage, mitigate, and monitor impacts |
| **Timing** | Conducted **before** project clearance | Implemented **during and after** project operation |
| **Output** | EIA Report with impact matrix and alternatives | Document with budget, responsibilities, and schedule |
| **Statutory** | Required under **EIA Notification 2006** | Part of EIA Report (Appendix) and a condition of EC |
| **Example** | Baseline study of ambient air around a fertilizer plant in Gujarat | Installing N₂O abatement catalyst + online stack monitor in the same plant |

> [!TIP]
> **Scoring tip (3 marks):** Definition (1) + Distinction table (1) + Example (1).

### **Q2.** *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]*
**"List any six environmental impacts of a hydroelectric project."**

**Model Answer:**

1. Submergence of forest, agricultural, and habitation land.
2. Displacement and rehabilitation of project-affected families.
3. Loss of aquatic biodiversity, including disruption of fish migration.
4. Reservoir-induced seismicity and downstream river bed degradation.
5. Greenhouse gas emissions (CH₄) from submerged biomass in tropical reservoirs.
6. Sediment trapping leading to downstream delta and coastal erosion.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024]*

**(a)** *Discuss the methodology of Environmental Impact Assessment of a **hydroelectric project** in India, highlighting the major impacts and mitigation measures.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Explain the concept of **Environmental Management Plan (EMP)** as applied to an **irrigation project**. Discuss its components with reference to waterlogging and secondary salinization.* **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Q A (a):**

The EIA of a hydroelectric project in India is governed by the **EIA Notification 2006 (amended 2009, 2020)** and the **Forest Conservation Act 1980**.

**Step 1 — Project Description and Site Context** *(1 mark)*
- A hydroelectric project typically consists of a dam/barrage, reservoir (if storage type), intake, headrace tunnel, penstock, powerhouse, tailrace, and transmission lines.
- Example: **Nathpa Jhakri (1500 MW, Satluj)** or **Tehri Dam (2400 MW, Bhagirathi)**.
- Identify the project category: HEPs > 50 MW are **Category A** (central appraisal by EAC).

**Step 2 — Baseline Data Collection** *(1 mark)*
Baseline data must cover:
- **Hydrology** — lean season flow, flood discharge, sediment load.
- **Aquatic ecology** — fish species, breeding grounds, migratory routes.
- **Forest & terrestrial ecology** — flora/fauna in submergence zone.
- **Socio-economic** — population, livelihoods, cultural sites in submergence.
- **Seismology** — historical earthquake data, tectonic features.
- **Air, water, noise, soil** — standard CPCB parameters for one season × 2 minimum.

**Step 3 — Impact Prediction** *(2 marks)*
Major impacts:
1. **Land submergence** — agricultural land, forest, settlements lost permanently.
2. **Displacement** — 1,000s of PAFs in large projects.
3. **Biodiversity loss** — habitat fragmentation, blocked fish migration.
4. **Reservoir-induced seismicity (RIS)** — particularly in tectonically active zones.
5. **Downstream flow alteration** — ecological and sediment-starvation effects.
6. **GHG emissions** — CH₄ from anaerobic decomposition of submerged biomass.
7. **Construction phase** — muck generation, blasting, worker camps.
8. **Reservoir rim instability** — landslides on steep slopes.

**Step 4 — Mitigation and EMP** *(2 marks)*
- **Catchment Area Treatment (CAT) Plan** — afforestation, check dams, terracing.
- **Compensatory Afforestation (CA)** — 1:1 to 2:1 ratio under FCA.
- **R&R Plan** as per RFCTLARR Act 2013 — full compensation, livelihood restoration.
- **Environmental flow (e-flow)** — minimum 30% of mean flow (Tennant) downstream.
- **Fish ladder/elevator** at the barrage.
- **Pre-impoundment biomass clearing** — to reduce CH₄.
- **Reservoir rim treatment** — bioengineering, drainage.
- **Muck disposal plan** — engineered, geotechnically stable dumpsites.
- **Seismic monitoring network** around the reservoir.

**Step 5 — Public Hearing and Clearance** *(1 mark)*
- Public hearing conducted by SPCB under EIA Notification.
- Concerns of downstream communities, oustees, fisherfolk addressed.
- Final EC issued by MoEF&CC with conditions.

---

#### **Solution to Q A (b):**

The **Environmental Management Plan (EMP)** of an irrigation project is a structured, time-bound, budgeted action plan covering all phases: pre-construction, construction, operation, and decommissioning.

**Step 1 — Components of an EMP** *(2 marks)*
A standard EMP has:
1. **Mitigation Plan** — specific measures for each significant impact.
2. **Monitoring Plan** — parameters, frequency, location, agency, cost.
3. **Institutional Plan** — who does what (project proponent, contractor, regulator).
4. **Budget** — capital and recurring EMP cost (typically 2–5% of project cost).
5. **Reporting & Compliance** — six-monthly compliance to MoEF&CC.
6. **Contingency Plan** — for dam break, canal breach, health outbreaks.
7. **Capacity Building & Training** — for farmers, operators, and staff.

**Step 2 — Specific Application to Waterlogging** *(2.5 marks)*
- **Cause:** Seepage from unlined canals + over-irrigation → rising water table.
- **Mitigation:** 
   - **Lining of canals** with PCC, HDPE, or geomembrane in permeable reaches.
   - **Subsurface drainage (SSD)** with perforated PVC laterals + collector drains.
   - **On-farm water management** — laser land leveling, border/basin/furrow irrigation.
   - **Crop selection** — water-efficient crops (pulses, millets) in high water-table zones.
- **Monitoring:** Observation wells (piezometers) at 1 km grid; electrical conductivity (EC) and SAR of groundwater.

**Step 3 — Specific Application to Secondary Salinization** *(2 marks)*
- **Cause:** Rising water table dissolves native salts; capillary rise deposits them at root zone.
- **Mitigation:**
   - **Drainage cum reuse** — saline drain water used for salt-tolerant forestry.
   - **Gypsum amendment** of sodic soils to replace Na⁺ with Ca²⁺.
   - **Salt-tolerant crop varieties** (e.g., CSR-30 rice, Kallar grass).
   - **Conjunctive use** of canal + groundwater to maintain water table.
- **Monitoring:** Soil EC, ESP, SAR at fixed sample points; groundwater EC.

**Step 4 — Long-term Institutional & Cost Aspects** *(0.5 mark)*
- **CADAs** (Command Area Development Authorities) to implement EMP in the operational phase.
- **Water Users' Associations (WUAs)** for participatory irrigation management.
- EMP cost = ~3–4% of project cost; recurring 0.3–0.5% per year.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Most students *list* EMP components but do not **link** each component to a specific impact (waterlogging / salinization). Marks for "specificity" are lost. Always write: *"For waterlogging, the mitigation is X, monitored at Y frequency, by Z agency, costing Rs. A."*

### **Question B (14 Marks)** *[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper 2024]*

**(a)** *Discuss the environmental impacts of a **fertilizer unit** (nitrogenous) and the Environmental Management Plan (EMP) to mitigate them.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *Compare the EIA approach for a **food processing industry** (sugar mill) and an **agricultural production unit**. What are the common mitigation measures for wastewater management in both?* **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to Q B (a):**

**Step 1 — Process Description of a Nitrogenous Fertilizer Unit** *(1 mark)*
- **Ammonia plant** — natural gas/naptha → steam reforming → CO shift → CO₂ removal → Haber-Bosch synthesis.
- **Urea plant** — NH₃ + CO₂ → ammonium carbamate → urea → prill/granule.
- **Utilities** — cooling tower, DM plant, captive power, steam generation.

**Step 2 — Environmental Impacts** *(2.5 marks)*

| Compartment | Impact | Pollutant |
| :- | :- | :- |
| Air | Stack emissions | NOₓ, SO₂, CO₂, NH₃ (fugitive), fluorides (if P-plant) |
| Air | Greenhouse gases | CO₂ (reforming), N₂O (nitric acid), CH₄ (minor) |
| Water | Process wastewater | NH₃, urea, oil, suspended solids |
| Land | Solid waste | Spent catalyst, gypsum, slag, sludge |
| Land | Hazardous waste | Arsenic sludge, spent mercury, oily sludge |
| Societal | Major accident hazard | NH₃ (Toxic), H₂SO₄, Cl₂ — explosion/toxic release |
| Societal | Odour | NH₃, amines, urea dust |

**Step 3 — EMP** *(2.5 marks)*

**Air emissions control:**
- Low-NOₓ burners; **SCR/SNCR** for NOₓ in nitric acid plants.
- **N₂O catalytic abatement** (e.g., secondary catalyst in nitric acid plant).
- **Ammonia scrubbing** for fugitive emissions.
- Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS) on stacks connected to CPCB.

**Water management:**
- Stripping column for NH₃ recovery from wastewater.
- Multi-effect evaporator for concentrate; **Zero Liquid Discharge (ZLD)**.
- Storm water management and oil-water separation.

**Solid & hazardous waste:**
- Secured landfill for hazardous waste as per **Hazardous Waste Management Rules 2016**.
- Recycling of spent catalysts (where techno-economically feasible).
- Fly ash utilization (brick, cement) from captive power.

**Safety & risk:**
- **On-Site Emergency Plan (OSEP)** and **Off-Site Emergency Plan (OSEP-2)** as per **MSIHC Rules 1989**.
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** every 5 years.
- **HAZOP** study of all process units.
- Ammonia tank farm with dyke, water spray, gas detection.

**Step 4 — Statutory and Monitoring** *(1 mark)*
- Environmental Clearance under EIA Notification 2006.
- Consent to Establish & Operate under Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981.
- Authorization under Hazardous Waste Rules 2016.
- Half-yearly compliance reports; annual environmental audit.

---

#### **Solution to Q B (b):**

**Step 1 — Sugar Mill EIA** *(2 marks)*
**Impacts:**
- **Wastewater:** Spent wash (BOD ~50,000 mg/L for distillery attached; ~3000 mg/L for sugar only).
- **Air emissions:** Boiler SPM, SO₂, NOₓ (bagasse/coal).
- **Solid waste:** Press mud (3–4% of cane), bagasse (30%), boiler ash.
- **Noise:** Crushers, centrifugal machines, DG sets.
- **Odour:** Fermentation, spent wash lagoons.
- **Land use:** Sugarcane cultivation upstream; landfill for sludge.

**Mitigation:**
- Biomethanation of spent wash (CHP — Combined Heat & Power from biogas).
- **Bagasse-based cogeneration** — renewable energy, reduces grid dependence.
- ETP with upflow anaerobic sludge blanket (UASB) reactor followed by activated sludge.
- Press mud composting with bio-inoculants.
- Rainwater harvesting and condensate recovery.

**Step 2 — Agricultural Production Unit EIA** *(2 marks)*
**Impacts:**
- **Soil:** Erosion, salinization, nutrient depletion, pesticide residue.
- **Water:** High abstraction, agro-chemical runoff, eutrophication.
- **Air:** Dust from ploughing, GHG (CH₄ from paddy, N₂O from N-fertilizers), VOCs from pesticide sprays.
- **Biodiversity:** Loss of agro-biodiversity, pollinator decline, soil microbe shift.
- **Social:** Occupational health, farmer indebtedness, water conflicts.

**Mitigation:**
- **Integrated Pest Management (IPM)** — biocontrol, pheromone traps, resistant varieties.
- **Integrated Nutrient Management (INM)** — FYM, vermicompost, biofertilizers.
- **Drip/sprinkler irrigation** — 30–50% water saving.
- **Crop rotation & cover crops** — break pest cycles, fix nitrogen.
- **Buffer zones** around water bodies; **riparian vegetation** restoration.
- **Organic certification** pathways (PGS-India, NPOP).

**Step 3 — Common Wastewater Management Strategy** *(2.5 marks)*
| Aspect | Sugar Mill | Agricultural Unit |
| :- | :- | :- |
| Source | Process + sanitation + boiler blowdown | Field drainage + farmyard wash + silage leachate |
| Nature | High BOD, biodegradable | Low BOD, high in N/P, pesticides |
| Treatment | UASB + activated sludge + polishing | Vegetated buffer strips + constructed wetlands + storage ponds |
| Disposal | Treated to land; ZLD preferred | Reuse for irrigation; constructed wetland outflow |
| Common Tools | Biological treatment, recycling | Constructed wetlands, riparian buffers |

**Common Measures:**
1. **Source reduction** — reduce water footprint at process level.
2. **Recycling & reuse** — treated wastewater for irrigation/land application.
3. **Constructed wetlands** — low-cost, low-energy polishing.
4. **Storm water management** — first-flush diversion.
5. **Monitoring** — BOD, COD, TSS, nutrients, pesticides, EC, SAR.
6. **Capacity building** — farmer training, operator training.

**Step 4 — Concluding Comparative Note** *(0.5 mark)*
While a sugar mill is a **point-source** industrial emitter, an agricultural unit is a **diffuse/non-point** source. EIA for the former is dominated by effluent treatment plant design; for the latter, by **landscape-level practices** (crop rotation, buffer zones, IPM).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Do **not** copy-paste a generic EMP. The evaluator specifically looks for **sector-specific** terminology: e.g., for fertilizer — "ammonia stripping column," "N₂O abatement," "OSEP"; for sugar mill — "biomethanation," "UASB," "cogeneration"; for agriculture — "IPM," "constructed wetland," "riparian buffer." Marks are reserved for **technical vocabulary** usage.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 4)**

### A. Universal EIA Framework (all 5 sectors)
- 8 stages: Screening → Scoping → Baseline → Impact Prediction → Alternatives → Mitigation + EMP → Public Hearing → Monitoring.
- Statutory: **EIA Notification 2006** (and its 2020 draft), **Water Act 1974, Air Act 1981, EP Act 1986, Forest Conservation Act 1980, Wildlife Protection Act 1972, RFCTLARR Act 2013, Hazardous Waste Rules 2016, MSIHC Rules 1989**.

### B. Sector-Wise High-Yield Points
- **Hydroelectric:** Submergence, displacement, RIS, e-flow, fish migration, CH₄, CAT plan, R&R plan.
- **Agricultural Production:** Soil degradation, agro-chemicals, IPM/INM, drip irrigation, agro-biodiversity, GHG (CH₄, N₂O).
- **Food Processing:** High BOD effluent, ETP, biomethanation, cogeneration, ZLD, composting, HACCP.
- **Fertilizer:** NH₃, NOₓ, N₂O, hazardous waste, OSEP, QRA, HAZOP, ammonia stripping, ZLD.
- **Irrigation:** Waterlogging, secondary salinization, conjunctive use, CAD, drainage, vector-borne disease, R&R, siltation.

### C. Numerical Formulas You Must Memorize
- $P = \eta \rho g Q H$ (hydro capacity)
- $DF = Q_r / (Q_r + Q_e)$
- Streeter–Phelps $t_c$ and $D_c$
- $L_p(r) = L_p(r_0) - 20 \log_{10}(r/r_0)$ (point source noise)
- $E_{annual} = P \times PLF \times 8760$
- Tennant e-flow: 30% mean flow minimum, 60% for good habitat.

### D. The Comparative Sectoral Impact Matrix (Section 2.8)
- Memorize the "Hi / Med / Lo" pattern. KTU's favorite 14-mark question is a **compare-and-contrast** across two of the five sectors.

### E. Key Statutory Authority
- Central: **MoEF&CC, EAC, CPCB**.
- State: **SEIAA, SEAC, SPCB**.

### F. Common Pitfalls to Avoid
1. Confusing **EIA** (predictive study) with **EMP** (action plan).
2. Forgetting to mention **public hearing** as a mandatory stage for Category A projects.
3. Citing the **wrong project capacity threshold** for Category A.
4. Ignoring **post-clearance monitoring** (six-monthly compliance).
5. Using generic "use renewable energy" without naming **specific measures** like "bagasse cogeneration" or "N₂O abatement."

### G. KTU Pattern Cheat Code
- For **3-mark** Part A: Definition + 2 specific sector examples.
- For **14-mark** Part B: Always structure as (i) Process/Project description → (ii) Baseline → (iii) Impacts → (iv) Mitigation/EMP → (v) Monitoring & Statutory.
- For **numerical** Part B (rare but possible): Use the standard formulas above.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute Mantra (before exam):**
> *"Five sectors, one framework, sector-specific language. The marks live in the specifics."*
